160:
Marcos’ numbers are not falling by accident. 

They are falling because people are tired. And sitting here, reading survey after survey, ang tanong ko na lang sa sarili ko is: kung fan ka ng presidenteng ito paano mo pa siya ipagtatanggol?

 Looking At Surveys and Public Opinion

There was a time I tried to give him the benefit of the doubt. 

Early days pa lang ng term niya, surveys said roughly three out of four Filipinos were satisfied with his performance. 

Honeymoon phase, fine. 

People were hopeful. Some of my own friends were like, “Baka naman he’ll be better than the father.”

Then I watch the numbers slide. 

By around early 2024, Pulse Asia was already showing his approval dropping from the high 70s to the mid‑50s, with disapproval doubling. 

Hindi ito chismis sa kanto. These are millions of people quietly changing their mind.

By the end of 2025, halos kalahati na ng adult Filipinos were saying they disapproved of his performance. 

SWS had his net trust at negative three, meaning mas marami na ang hindi nagtitiwala kaysa nagtitiwala. 

When I read that, I caught myself thinking: “So it’s not just me. Hindi ako nag-iinarte lang.”

When I look at those numbers, I don’t see politics. Nakikita ko mga taong gaya ko na quietly umaatras.

He started with all that goodwill. Now he’s underwater. Hindi siya inabot doon nang walang dahilan.

 Ano ba talaga kinagagalit ng tao?

Whenever I check the surveys, I notice something very simple. 

Wala namang masyadong arte yung hinihingi ng mga Pilipino. The top concerns keep repeating: inflation, corruption, low wages, poverty, jobs.

I think of friends na kalahati ng sahod napupunta sa groceries, of parents na hindi makapag-enroll ng anak sa private kahit gusto nila. I look at my own budget and feel that slow squeeze sa presyo ng pagkain at kuryente.

And then sa balita, what do we see?

- Corruption stories around big-ticket projects and agencies. 
- Familiar names popping up around these projects. 
- “Investigations” na parang pampabango lang, mabagal, selective. 
- A president who seems more irritated with critics than with the corruption itself.

Analysts say corruption has now climbed right behind high prices as a top concern. I do not need an analyst to tell me that. I can feel it in conversations, in the “hay naku” sa group chats, in the way people roll their eyes when government is mentioned.

So when I see his ratings drop, hindi ako nagtataka. 

It feels like the country is saying out loud what a lot of us have been thinking quietly: pagod na kami.

 The cabinet reshuffle that feels fake

Then came the dramatic move: courtesy resignations, cabinet “reset,” post‑midterms drama. For a second, I wanted to believe. Maybe, I thought, this is the turning point.

But when I looked closely, ang dami palang na-retain.

- Economic team mostly stayed. 
- Loyal allies stayed. 
- Some were just moved from one post to another, parang musical chairs. 
- A few were replaced, then recycled into other roles.

As a writer, I care about patterns. And the pattern I see is this: ang lakas sa gesture, ang hina sa substance.

Commentators were already warning that if policies do not change, the reshuffle is just image work. And honestly, that’s how it felt to me. Parang nilipat-lipat lang yung upuan sa lumulubog na barko.

Sabi ko nga sa post kahapon: nasusunog yung bahay mo pero ang naiisip mong solusyon is to rearrange the furniture in the living room. 

Seriously, WTF, right? 🤷‍♂️

He treats it like a staffing problem. 

We are living a policy problem.

 My main problem: half-baked siya

This is where I really lose patience with him. 

My problem with Marcos is his actions are always half-baked. Laging bitin. Laging safe.

The clearest example for me is the whole mess with unprogrammed funds.

For 2025 and 2026, Congress loaded the budget with massive “unprogrammed appropriations” — hundreds of billions in standby funds. Matagal na itong binabatikos, kasi these pots are super flexible and super prone to pork and ghost projects.

Then, late 2025, a Supreme Court justice basically says what a lot of us have been thinking. 

Justice Ramon Paul Hernando calls unprogrammed appropriations, in any form, unconstitutional — riders with no clear funding sources, breeding grounds for corruption. 

After that, lawmakers and lawyers start talking about challenging them before the Court.

So what does Marcos do?

- He signs the 2025 budget but vetoes around P194 billion in items, including some unprogrammed funds. 
- He signs the 2026 budget, then vetoes P92.5 billion in unprogrammed items, reportedly seven out of ten unprogrammed lines. 
- Then he goes out and acts like this proves his commitment to transparency and fiscal discipline.

And here’s where I just stare at the news and mutter to myself: 

So alam mong may constitutional issue, pero nagtira ka pa?

If you truly believe these unprogrammed funds are constitutionally problematic, bakit hindi mo ni‑veto lahat? If the entire structure is sick, why are you so proud you removed just a slice and left the rest?

Makabayan called his P92.5‑billion veto a “token gesture,” pointing out that plenty of unprogrammed items remain in the 2026 budget and that big, graft‑prone items were spared. 

Analysts noted the same thing earlier: even after his 2025 veto, pork and opaque funds remained huge in key agencies like DPWH.

As a citizen, it makes me feel insulted. 

He wants applause for doing the bare minimum.

Kung mali, alisin mo. 
Kung tama, ipaglaban mo. 

Ang ginawa niya: admit na may problema, then keep just enough of it to stay politically comfortable. That is the pattern that keeps bothering me.

 Token gestures, real damage

On paper, the numbers look big: P194 billion vetoed here, P92.5 billion there. Malacañang talks about transparency, some senators praise the veto. But when I zoom out, what I see is a familiar script:

- The unprogrammed framework survives. 
- Pork and flexible funds survive. 
- The Supreme Court still has to clean up what Malacañang refused to fully confront.

Ordinary people do not read veto messages and concurring opinions. 

But we feel patterns.

We see a president who:

- Boasts about vetoing billions that probably should not have been there in the first place. 
- Flirts with the idea that these funds are constitutionally questionable. 
- Still signs budgets that keep a chunk of those same funds intact.

That’s when I realized: 

This is a deliberate choice to do just enough to claim credit, but not enough to fix the system.

Half-baked. All the time.

Masabi lang na may ginagawa. 

 The thing he won’t say out loud

Kung titingnan mo, consistent siya sa isang bagay: ayaw niyang umamin.

If midterms go badly, he looks at his cabinet. 
If surveys sink, he talks about misinformation or critics. 

If corruption stories explode, he talks about “investigations” but rarely burns bridges with powerful allies.

By the end of 2025, surveys painted a clear picture:

- His disapproval rising to around the high 40s. 
- His approval shrinking to the low to mid‑30s. 
- His net trust sitting in the negatives, while other big names still post much higher positive trust.

You don’t need to be a fan of any of those other politicians to understand what people are saying here. They’re comparing energy, decisiveness, and backbone. And in that comparison, Marcos looks weak.

As someone who writes about politics, I find myself repeating one question: 

Saan ka ba talaga willing makipagsabong, Mr. President?

Because right now, it looks like you’ll fight for optics, photo ops, and partial vetoes. But when it comes to fully cleaning up budgets, cutting off corrupt allies, or admitting you got something wrong, biglang wala.

 Why this keeps me up at night

Ito yung nakakainis: some people say, “Wala namang re‑election, tapusin na lang natin six years, tiis na lang.” 

Pero hindi ganun kasimple.

When a president’s trust collapses:

- Every new policy is questioned, even the good ones. 
- Anti‑corruption efforts look selective and insincere. 
- International partners sense weakness and push harder in negotiations. 
- Local politicians start hedging and playing their own games.

And underneath all of that, there’s this quiet fear I feel from people: 

“If we can’t even trust him to be decisive and honest about money, what happens when the next big crisis hits?”

Another pandemic. A regional conflict. A food crisis. A climate disaster. 
Pumili ka. Or lahat sabay-sabay.

Low trust at the top makes every disaster worse. And that’s why these half-baked decisions are not just annoying to me as a writer and a Filipino. They’re dangerous.

 What I wish he’d do (but doubt he will)

Kung sapilitan akong maging hopeful, ito yung gusto kong makita:

- A real, public break with compromised allies, not just quiet reassignments. 
- Clear, measurable targets on inflation and wages, with regular honest updates, not spin. 
- Full honesty about mistakes: “Nagkamali ako. Babaguhin natin.” 
- A decision to kill unprogrammed appropriations as a class, not just trim them, and to force Congress to legislate a clean replacement. 
- Less travel, more visible work and presence in moments of crisis.

None of these are impossible. 

But they all require something he has not shown so far: the willingness to lose something — an ally, some pork, a bit of political comfort — in order to do the right thing.

So yes, his approval is sliding. His trust rating is negative. People are tired, angry, and over it. 

And here I am, staring at another cabinet reshuffle, another partial veto, another speech, thinking out loud:

The problem is not the surveys, or the cabinet, or even just the budget line items.

The problem is a president who is always half-baked when the country needs someone fully in.

Sabi ko dati, Duterte set the bar so low that he practically made Marcos look like a hero. 

Looking back at that old line now, ang kaya ko na lang sabihin: sige, fine, maybe the bar moved a few inches higher under him, but that’s it, doon lang umangat. 

There are only a few years left before 2028 and he is term‑limited, and unless something close to a miracle happens, he is not going to turn into the kind of president this country actually needs. 

The way things are going, those last two years of his term will not be a season of courage or clarity, just a long, awkward stretch where everyone can see it already: he is nothing more than a sitting duck waiting to be kicked out.

Disclaimer: 

This piece is not a call for Marcos to resign. Kung tutuusin, mas kadiri at mas delikado pa yung mga pwedeng ipalit sa kanya sa ngayon. 

This is a critique, an opinion piece, grounded in how I feel as a citizen and a writer, and in the data and reports I have been reading. 

It is frustration on paper, not a roadmap for ousting anyone.
161:
Mathematics of Greed: The Cong-tractor Economy of Edwin Gardiola 

1,193. 

That is the number of bank accounts the Court of Appeals ordered frozen in connection with one congressman and his contractor network, after the Anti-Money Laundering Council said those accounts may be tied to possible violations of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act. 

Those 1,193 accounts sit inside a bigger picture: as of the latest order, total frozen assets in the flood control scandal have reached around P20.3 billion. 

These are bank accounts that accumulated from individual frozen financial lifelines, each one holding a piece of a money trail that had to be layered, moved, and hidden over years. 

A thousand accounts. Plus 193 more. 

I keep repeating that because this is what the Lower House ignored and tolerated for years - because they protect each other. 

Let's start with this: Rep. Edwin Gardiola is the lawmaker linked to those accounts. 

 The Contractor That Became Congress 

Gardiola wasn’t always in government. 

He made his money in construction, the kind that lives and dies on contracts from the Department of Public Works and Highways. 

From 2000 to 2008, during the Arroyo years, two family firms alone, JSG Construction and E. Gardiola Construction, reportedly secured around P11.6 billion in DPWH projects: 

- about P4.42 billion for JSG Construction, co-owned with his wife Judy

- P7.21 billion for E. Gardiola Construction, owned by his brother Elmer and sister-in-law Elaine. 

Those contracts made his family one of the top DPWH contractors of that era. 

On paper, it looked like regular government contracting: private firms doing public works. In reality, it was already building the foundation of something much bigger. 

In 2012, the Bureau of Internal Revenue filed tax evasion charges against him. One detail from that case says everything. 

In 2006, Gardiola declared a gross taxable income of about P769,808 and paid only P1,856 in income tax. 

In the same year, he bought a 2003 BMW 745i11 for around P4.75 million. Between 2006 and 2009, records from the Land Transportation Office showed he bought several luxury vehicles, with total vehicle purchases reaching about P30.38 million, while he failed to even file income tax returns for 2007, 2008, and 2009. 

Imagine: while ordinary Filipinos quietly, religiously, consistently, and honestly pay their taxes, one rich, powerful construction magnate was allegedly cheating the system.

 From Contractor to “Cong-tractor” 

Fast forward to 2022. After all those years of contracts and a live tax case on record, Gardiola enters the House of Representatives as the party-list representative of Construction Workers Solidarity.

The man whose family firms made billions off public works now becomes a lawmaker in a position to influence which projects get prioritized and funded. 

By then, the construction empire had changed form but not nature. 

After the old JSG and E. Gardiola Construction era, new companies connected to him and his relatives show up: Newington Builders Inc., Laurel Corp., and S-Ang Construction & General Trading. 

From 2016 to 2025, DPWH data show that companies linked to him secured around P39.78 billion in public works contracts:

- about P20.34 billion for Newington Builders - around P3.03 billion for Laurel
- roughly P16.40 billion for S-Ang. 

In total, they reportedly landed 358 contracts: 

- 188 for Newington
- 27 for Laurel 
- 143 for S-Ang 

That’s how the “cong-tractor” label follows him around. 

Not just a contractor. 
Not just a congressman.

A congressman whose orbit of companies keeps winning public infrastructure work year after year after year.

 The Number That Changed Everything 

On July 22, 2025, in his State of the Nation Address, President Marcos ordered a crackdown on anomalous flood control projects. 

Months later, investigations pointed to ghost or non-existent projects, padded contracts, and a familiar set of names. 

By November 2025, an investigative body recommended graft and plunder charges against Gardiola and several other lawmakers. 

One key allegation from Batangas Rep. Leandro Leviste: that Gardiola had “pre-ordered” around P22 billion worth of projects, lining up flood control and infrastructure work even before proper congressional review of the budget could run its course. 

Then the Court of Appeals stepped in. 

First came earlier freeze orders in 2025. Then, in December 2025, a new order specifically targeted around 1,193 bank accounts tied to Gardiola and major contractors. 

Across all related orders, frozen assets in the flood control scandal totaled about P20.3 billion. 

The AMLC also flagged that, overall, the CA had issued freeze orders covering thousands more accounts and other assets in connection with the same probe. 

One man’s name is loud in the headlines. But it takes an entire ecosystem to generate 1,193 accounts and billions in contracts. 

 The Pattern Nobody Stopped 

The part that's difficult to swallow is how long this pattern lived in public view. 

Back in 2009, the Philippine Center for Investigative Journalism was already reporting that JSG and E. Gardiola Construction were top DPWH contractors with billions in projects under Arroyo. 

In 2012, the BIR publicly charged him with attempting to evade taxes and failing to file returns while buying luxury vehicles like BMW, Lexus, Jaguar, and others. 

Between 2011 and 2017, while those issues hung in the background, Gardiola and his wife acquired three properties in Southern California. 

One was a Newport Beach mansion, estimated at close to P1 billion, a six-bedroom, nine-bathroom house on an 18,000-plus square foot lot overlooking the coast. 

The other two were homes in Ontario and Fontana, still under his name according to US property records. 

All of this existed: the DPWH contract history, the BIR complaints, the US properties. 

Yet in 2022, he wins a House seat. 

In 2022 alone, as he entered Congress, Newington Builders reportedly secured around P1.9 billion in DPWH contracts. 

That’s the pattern: 

- Billions in contracts from 2000 to 2008. 
- Luxury vehicles and undeclared income from 2006 to 2009. 
- Three US properties bought between 2011 and 2017. 
- Billions more in contracts from 2016 to 2025 through new firms. 
- A congressional seat in 2022. 
- A presidential order to investigate in 2025. 
- And only then, in late 2025, the 1,193 frozen bank accounts. 

 The Real Crime 

Those 1,193 frozen accounts are the forensic aftermath. 

They show where money went, who moved it, which entities were used. But the deeper crime is how long the system lived comfortably with all the red flags. 

When a man can go from P769,808 in declared income and P1,856 in tax, to buying P30.38 million worth of luxury cars, to accumulating at least P11.6 billion in contracts under one administration, then another P39.78 billion under the next years, and still end up as a party-list representative for “construction workers,” something larger than one man is broken. 

The freeze order is the state finally saying “enough” on paper. But it came after decades of “go ahead” in practice.

So, think about it. 

You, the ordinary taxpayer, watching your salary get sliced by withholding tax every month, lining up for BIR deadlines, paying VAT on everything from sardines to school supplies, doing all of that quietly, religiously, consistently, and honestly. 

Meanwhile, a rich, powerful construction magnate with billions in government contracts and three houses in California somehow “forgets” to file income tax returns for years, buys luxury cars worth over P30 million, and ends up with 1,193 bank accounts frozen on suspicion of corruption. 

And for the longest time, the system still treated him better than it treats you. 

So question them. 

Every congressman, every senator, every DPWH official who talks about “projects” and “development” while floods keep rising and your taxes keep climbing. 

Do not accept their statements at face value, do not swallow their press releases as truth, and do not be ashamed of your anger. 

Be angry, be very angry, because these people are stealing from you in the open, and almost no one, least of all their friends in Congress or the Senate, is doing anything real to stop it.

162:
What kind of garbage is this, Greco Belgica?

There are days when you scroll your feed, see a screenshot, and literally say out loud: 
"Nasaan ang utak ng taong ito?”

Greco Belgica’s open letter to Donald J. Trump, begging him to “help bring home” Rodrigo Duterte from the ICC, is one of those posts. 

It’s the kind of thing that makes you question if these people still believe in law, or if they just believe in their own myths and each other’s ego.

 The legal nonsense

Let’s start with the part Belgica is pretending this is about: the law.

He’s asking Trump—now the US president again—to intervene in the detention of Duterte, who is currently under ICC custody in The Hague, facing charges of crimes against humanity linked to the drug war. 

The problem is simple: the United States has no legal authority over the ICC. None. 

The US is not a party to the Rome Statute, never joined the court, and has no standing in Duterte’s case.

Trump can scream, tweet, sign executive orders, and sanction ICC officials, and he has already done that. 

In 2020, and again under his new term via Executive Order 14203, his administration imposed sanctions and visa bans against ICC staff as punishment for daring to investigate US allies. 

That move was widely condemned by human rights organizations and the court itself as an attack on the global rule of law.

Pero kahit gaano kalakas ang sigaw niya, that doesn’t translate into legal power over the ICC. It just shows his contempt for accountability.

The ICC has jurisdiction over Duterte because the alleged crimes happened while the Philippines was still a state party to the Rome Statute, from 2011 to 2019. 

The Philippines’ withdrawal in 2019 does not erase crimes committed during that period. 

That’s settled in ICC practice and explained by international and local legal experts.

So on the legal level, Belgica’s appeal is exactly what it is: garbage. 

A legally empty gesture made to look like “concern for sovereignty.”

 He’s not appealing to law. He’s appealing to Trump’s chaos.

Pero to be honest, hindi ko rin siya nakikita as a legal appeal.

What Belgica is really doing is appealing to Trump’s character: his unpredictability, his disregard for institutions, his comfort with breaking norms if it serves his own side. 

Trump is the same politician who sanctioned the ICC, attacked judges, and called any investigation into him or his allies a witch hunt.

He is not known for his deep respect for courts and treaties. He is known for trying to bulldoze them.

So when Belgica writes:

“BUT, WE ARE NOT ASKING FOR THE US TO ARREST OUR SITTING PRESIDENT. INSTEAD, WE ASK FOR YOUR HELP IN BRINGING HOME THE FORMER PRESIDENT ARRESTED AND DETAINED BY THE ICC. OUR GOAL IS FOR BOTH THE CURRENT AND FORMER LEADERS TO FACE JUSTICE IN OUR OWN PHILIPPINE COURTS. WE WANT TO RESTORE THE RULE OF LAW WITHIN OUR OWN BORDERS.”

He’s not talking to “President of the United States, constrained by law and treaties.” 
He’s talking to “Trump, destroyer of institutions, patron saint of grievance politics.”

He’s appealing to Trump’s absence of logic. To the man who sanctions a court for doing its job. To the man who has no respect for democratic and legal institutions when they get in the way of his people.

And in that very specific respect, tama yung pakiramdam na Belgica, Trump, and Duterte are the same.

Not identical lives. Not identical contexts. But the same allergy to accountability. The same instinct to treat courts, media, and international bodies as enemies, not as guardrails. The same belief that rules are flexible as long as you are on the “right” side.

 The drug war they’re trying to sanitize

Belgica tries to justify this stunt by invoking the usual Greatest Hits: drugs, corruption, West Philippine Sea, rule of law.

Let’s talk about the drug war first.

By the end of Duterte’s term, official police counts acknowledged thousands of people killed in anti-drug operations. 

Human rights groups and academic projects like Dahas UP put the estimate much higher when you include vigilante-style killings. 

UP’s own human rights advocates point out that thousands of families were left with no justice, no proper investigation, and no clear path to accountability—one major reason why the ICC became their last resort.

So when Belgica frames Duterte as some kind of necessary “solution” to drugs, he’s not telling the whole story. 

The drug war didn’t solve the drug problem. It normalized killing as policy, and it attracted the ICC’s attention because the pattern of violence looked systematic and state-tolerated.

Ito yung “hero” na gusto niyang sagipin.

 The “rule of law” that never protected victims

Belgica says the goal is “for both the current and former leaders to face justice in our own Philippine courts” and to “restore the rule of law within our own borders.”

That sounds nice. It also sounds fake.

The ICC is only in the picture because domestic mechanisms failed. 

For years, the Philippine government either dismissed or stonewalled calls to investigate extrajudicial killings linked to the drug war. 

Official investigations were limited, selective, and often focused on low-level police. High-ranking officials were insulated.

The complementarity principle is clear: the ICC steps in only when a state is unwilling or unable to genuinely prosecute grave crimes. 

That’s the legal way of saying, “You had your chance. You didn’t do it. Now the world steps in.”

On top of that, public opinion is not on Belgica’s side. 

Surveys in 2025 showed that about half of Filipinos agree Duterte should be held accountable before the ICC for drug war deaths, with support strongest in NCR, Visayas, and Balance Luzon. 

Only a minority openly object. People are watching the case, and many want accountability—not escape.

So when Belgica uses “rule of law” and “the will of the people” as his anchor, ang totoo niyan, he’s using those words to protect a man the law has finally caught up with.

 What Belgica’s appeal really says about him

Honest read: yung ginagawa niya, walang kinalaman sa batas kundi para sa tribong DDS at para kay Tatay Digs. 

Belgica was Duterte’s ally, a former PACC chair, a political loyalist deeply tied into that ecosystem. 

He’s now writing to Trump, another leader known for attacking courts and the ICC, trying to build a moral corridor from Malacañang to Mar-a-Lago.

The message under the message is simple:

Courts are fine when they go after our enemies. 

Courts are “overreach” when they go after the heroes in our camp 
 
If the law hurts our side, let’s call in a bigger bully to smash the law.

That’s why the whole thing feels like garbage. 

Not just because it’s legally baseless, but because it reveals something bulok about how these men see power and influence: 

Institutions are tools. 
Justice is negotiable. 
Victims are collateral.

What Belgica is really defending is not Duterte’s innocence. 
He’s defending Duterte’s impunity.

Madaling sabihin na, “Eh post lang yan sa Facebook. Bakit kailangan bigyan ng pansin?” 

Because this kind of messaging does damage. 

It teaches people that international courts are villains when they hold powerful men to account, that accountability is “anti-Filipino,” that sovereignty means we only like the law when we control it.

The truth is the opposite.

And internationally? It’s fucking embarassing. 

Baka isipin ng mga nakakabasa na bobo at hindi marunong mag isip, mag basa, at mag research man lang ang mga Pilipino. 

Sovereignty without accountability is just a fancy word for impunity. 

A state that refuses to hold its own powerful people accountable is a state that abandons its citizens to abuse. 

The ICC is there precisely for situations like this—when thousands are dead, domestic systems have failed, and the victims’ last hope is a court outside their own borders.

So yes, what Belgica posted is garbage.

Legal garbage. Historical garbage. Moral garbage.

And the most insulting part is not that he wrote it. It’s that he expects us to swallow it.

163:
When “I’m Not Aware” Becomes An Insult

Joel Villanueva says the charges against him are “ridiculous.”

Not “fabricated.” Not “here’s my evidence.” Just ridiculous. As if the shocking part here is not the P150 million allegedly delivered to a rest house in his hometown, but the fact that anyone dares to question him.

If you are a tax-paying Filipino, that should piss you off. That is your money being treated like Monopoly cash in somebody else’s private game.

 The Genius Who Knew Nothing

Here’s the story: a former DPWH engineer says 600 million pesos in flood control projects were lined up, and 150 million of that ended up as kickback, supposedly delivered to a rest house in Bocaue, Bulacan.

Same town Joel is from. Same province he loves to brand as “his” area. Same projects that magically appeared under his name.

And yet his line is simple: he didn’t know.

He claims he requested multi-purpose buildings, not flood control. So when those turned into flood control projects, became part of a ghost-works scandal, and got traced back to him, the senator was, apparently, just as shocked as everyone else.

Everything was happening right beside his political and literal backyard, but sure, he knew nothing.

 This Is Not His First “Amnesia”

This whole “I didn’t know” act is not new.

Years ago, the Ombudsman under Conchita Carpio Morales ordered his dismissal over pork barrel funds linked to ghost projects and a bogus NGO. His excuse? His signatures were forged. Again, he “didn’t know.”

Morales looked at the records and basically said: no, you’re lying. She found him guilty of serious dishonesty and more.

Then a later Ombudsman reversed that decision. Suddenly, he was clean. Case gone, problem solved.

Fast forward: Morales speaks up and says she stands by her original findings, and that the process used to clear him never even reached her. In short, the system cleaned him up on paper. The facts didn’t magically change.

Now here we are again, with a completely different scandal, and he’s still playing the same role: the powerful man who’s always “shocked,” “hurt,” and “not aware.”

How many times can one person benefit from “I didn’t know” before we admit that this is not innocence, it’s a tactic?

 “I Didn’t Know” Is Not Harmless

Let’s be brutally honest.

If he’s telling the truth and he honestly had no idea that hundreds of millions in projects were being arranged under his name, in his hometown, that alone should disqualify him from public office. You cannot be that clueless and still pretend to be competent.

If he’s lying, then he’s hiding behind ignorance because it’s the one thing that’s hard to disprove. You can’t subpoena someone’s brain and pull out a file called “kickbacks.”

That’s why politicians love this defense. No receipts, no screenshots, just “I didn’t know.” It’s the safest line money can buy.

Meanwhile, taxpayers are supposed to swallow that. Flood victims are supposed to swallow that. Voters are supposed to swallow that.

 Why This Should Offend You

Every time a politician gets away with “I didn’t know,” the message is simple: as long as you’re high enough in the food chain, you can always pretend to be stupid and the system will treat it as innocence.

Villanueva is not some random barangay official. He is a senator, a former majority leader, someone who lectures others about lies and accountability when it suits him.

And yet when it’s his turn in the hot seat, suddenly he knows nothing, remembers nothing, and is simply the victim of a ridiculous case.

If you work, pay taxes, and try to live within the rules, you are funding this circus. You are paying for salaries, offices, security, staff, and, apparently, very expensive performances of “I had no idea.”

That should insult you.

 The One Thing We Can Still Do

We can’t control the DOJ, the Ombudsman, or the backroom deals.

But we can remember.

We can remember that this is not the first time Joel Villanueva’s name has been linked to ghost projects. 
We can remember that “I didn’t know” is his favorite shield. 
We can remember that every peso they treat like a joke could have been food, medicine, classrooms, flood control that actually works.

So the next time he appears on TV sounding righteous about corruption and deception, at least you’ll know the script he uses when the spotlight turns back on him:

“I’m not aware.”

And you can decide if you’re still willing to accept that from someone whose job is to know.

The Filipino Paradox: When You're Complaining About Corruption But You Are Corrupt Yourself (And You Don't See It)

[An MCT Reflection]

Lately, this has been bothering me more than usual. The more I scroll, the more one thing keeps screaming in my head.

There's this thing we do.

We hate corruption. We really do. We post about it on Facebook. We rant about politicians in group chats. We shake our heads at news of ghost projects and missing billions. We say things like "Nakakapagod na," or "Wala na talaga," with that specific tone of exhaustion that only comes from watching the same story unfold over and over again.

And then we slip that traffic enforcer two hundred pesos to avoid the ticket.

And we don't see the connection.

 The Disconnect We Live In

Surveys show that nearly all Filipinos—97 percent—believe corruption in government is widespread. Fighting corruption consistently ranks as one of the top concerns when people are asked what needs to change. For many, it's the second or third most urgent issue after jobs and the cost of living. 

We say we don't want corrupt leaders.

Then we vote for them anyway.

We complain about the billions stolen through flood control scams. 

We rage about politicians pocketing kickbacks while communities drown. We call them out, rightfully, for their greed and shamelessness.

But when the same motorist who shares anti-corruption posts gets pulled over, the calculation is instant: pay the bribe, or waste half a day at City Hall. 

When someone's cousin needs a fixer to speed up a government document, we give them the number. 

When elections come and someone offers cash for our vote, some of us take it and rationalize: "Ninakaw naman nila yan sa atin in the first place."

Small corruption. Petty corruption. The kind we don't even call corruption anymore.

We call it diskarte.

 The Corruption We Can't See

What I keep thinking about is this: we've created different categories of corruption in our heads. 

There's the big, bad corruption, the billions-of-pesos kind that makes us angry. 

And then there's everything else, the small compromises we make every day, which we don't count as corruption at all.

This is the Filipino paradox.

We hate corruption in the abstract. But we tolerate it, participate in it, even justify it in the specific.

And the really dangerous part? We genuinely don't see ourselves as corrupt. 

We see ourselves as victims of a broken system who have no choice but to play along to survive.

I'm not exempt from this. I wish I could say I'm just an observer, but hindi. I've also chosen the shortcut before, told myself, "Wala namang mawawala," and moved on like it was nothing.

We tell ourselves stories. "Everyone does it." "I'm not hurting anyone." "The system forced me to do this." "If I don't, someone else will." These are how we justify our little corruption that let us sleep at night, that let us see ourselves as trapped rather than complicit.

Now, multiply this mindset by millions and you realize why severe corruption doesn't just happen here. It's sustained by all of us, every day, with every small choice. That's why it won't go away anytime soon.

 How We Got Here

This didn't start yesterday.

Corruption in the Philippines is systemic because it's already baked into how services are delivered, how institutions operate, and how we've been conditioned to interact with power. 

When you grow up seeing that connections or bribes speed up services, you learn that playing by the rules means being punished with delays. 

When following the legal process takes months but a bribe cuts it to days, a lot of people choose the shortcut.

We've inherited a culture where palakasan, who you know, matters more than what's right. Where utang na loob, pakikisama, and family loyalty, beautiful values at their core, get twisted to justify exploitation.

Those values themselves are not the problem. The problem is the scale and where we apply them.

Utang na loob within a family or close circle can build real bonds. But when political patrons use utang na loob to demand loyalty in exchange for ghost projects, it becomes corruption wearing the mask of gratitude. 

Pakikisama keeps communities together. But when bureaucrats enable plunder because they don't want to rock the boat with colleagues, hindi na yan pakikisama, corruption na ang tawag diyan. 

The same person who shares their last peso with their barkada can enable systems that impoverish millions. Strong micro-solidarity. Weak macro-solidarity.

I see this even in my own circles. We're so good to the people in front of us. But when it comes to the faceless "public," suddenly our standards become flexible. People we know get favors, shortcuts, the benefit of the doubt. People we don't know get whatever's left, or nothing.

We're good to the people we can see. We're indifferent to everyone else.

 The Everyday Corruption We Normalize

Let's name what this looks like in daily life.

Traffic violations. Instead of accepting the ticket, drivers routinely hand enforcers cash. The enforcers expect it. The drivers expect it. It's become a transaction so normal that rejecting it feels almost rebellious. Authorities have even had to launch campaigns begging motorists to stop bribing them, because the practice has become so entrenched that refusing feels weird.

Fixers. They exist because our processes are complicated on purpose. Someone profits from the confusion. And we pay them because the alternative is bureaucratic hell.

Vote-buying. Cash handed out before elections, sometimes thousands per household. Field experiments have documented this clearly: money flows during campaign season, and people take it because they need it. People take it and tell themselves, "Kailangan natin ng pera ngayon. At least may makukuha tayo." Survival logic disguised as pragmatism.

Falsifying documents. Workers taking home office supplies. Applicants padding resumes. Government employees cutting corners. Small acts, individually. But together, they feed the same culture of dishonesty we claim to despise.

And then there are the quieter, more personal versions.

There's the church leader who posts long rants about corrupt officials but proudly shares stories of how a relative "fixed" their land title through a backdoor arrangement. The story is told as a win, not a warning. Parang achievement.

There's the office manager who complains about trapo politics but passes envelopes to staff during campaign season, reminding them, subtly, which candidate "helped the company" with permits. No one says the word, but everyone knows what's going on.

As someone who writes about politics, I feel the tension here. Ang dali to sound righteous on the page and forget the small compromises off the page. Ang dali maging matapang sa analysis, pero tahimik sa corrupt na diskarte.

And we've been doing this for so long that many of us don't even feel guilty about it anymore. For some, corruption has moved from shameful to simply normal, just how things work.

 The Hypocrisy We Can't Name

I've seen this up close. People who rage about corruption online but benefit from it offline. Activists and politicians with spotless speeches who take advantage of the same systems they critique. Government workers who advocate for good governance while pocketing extra on the side.

There's this kind of person you probably know: mabait, generous, active in church or community, very loud about corrupt politicians. They share every exposé, every quote about honesty and integrity. Seven out of ten of us say we prefer candidates who fight corruption. We really do want them.

But during enrollment season, they call a friend "inside" the school so their child can skip the line and secure a slot over someone more qualified. During elections, they coordinate the distribution of money in their barangay, saying, "Ganito naman talaga every election, at least matulungan ang mga tao."

If you ask them, they don't see themselves as part of the problem. They see themselves as practical. Realistic. Street-smart. 

And to be honest, I've caught myself in the same mindset. That little voice that says, "Ganyan talaga dito," or "Kung hindi ko gagawin, ako rin ang dehado."

We say we want change. But when we get the chance to be part of corruption, we take it. Because many times, we're not against corruption. We're against being left out of it.

Madalas, hindi talaga korapsyon ang ikinagagalit natin, kundi yung pakiramdam na wala tayong nakuha samantalang sila, meron. Unfair nga naman. Corrupt silang lahat tapos ikaw hindi. 

 Why We Keep Voting For The Corrupt

If most of us say we hate corruption, why do corrupt politicians keep winning?

Because we're not issue-oriented. We're personality-oriented, relationship-oriented, survival-oriented. 

We vote based on who we know, who gave us something, who shares our last name or hometown or religion. 

Candidates with scandals and corruption allegations still lead surveys because they come from families we know, or because they've delivered something tangible to us before.

We'd rather vote for someone from our tribe, someone who gave us a bag of rice or helped with a hospital bill, than an outsider with a clean record and good intentions.

I think about conversations I've had with relatives before elections. You lay out the facts, the track record, the cases. And they still say, "Eh tumulong yan sa atin noon." End of discussion. The math is already done in their head, and it's not about who's honest. It's about who owes them something and who they owe something to.

And here's the bitter twist: decades of corruption trained us to think this way. 

When you assume all politicians are corrupt anyway, you vote for the one who might at least give you something. 

You vote for the kurap who gets things done over the honest one who gets blocked at every turn. 

We've stopped expecting integrity. We've started expecting only efficiency and patronage.

The system has slowly lowered our standards.

 The Moral Blindness We've Developed

There's this idea called moral disengagement. It's how we convince ourselves that wrong actions are acceptable in certain situations. It's the psychological trick that lets us do something wrong while still seeing ourselves as good people.

We shift responsibility: "I didn't have a choice." "The system made me do it." "Everyone expects it."

We minimize consequences: "It's just a small thing." "No one really gets hurt." "It's not like I'm stealing billions."

We normalize it: "Everyone does it." "Ganito na talaga dito." We even celebrate it sometimes, as hustle, as survival instinct, as street smarts.

The scary thing is, I see this in myself too. The moments I explain away a small lie. The times I keep quiet when someone I like benefits from something shady, just because I don't want conflict. The way I post about accountability while protecting people close to me.

And the more we engage in these small acts, the easier it becomes to justify the next one. The first bribe is uncomfortable. The tenth feels routine. We stop noticing the gap between what we say and what we do because the gap has become so familiar.

We gradually build a fake version of ourselves, someone comfortable with compromise. Over time, that fake version becomes our default. We forget how it felt to have clear lines.

 The Way Forward: Starting With Ourselves

I'm not here to bash people; I'm trying to be honest about the kind of system we've all gotten used to.

We can't fight corruption out there if we're participating in it in here.

We can't demand accountability from politicians while bribing traffic enforcers. We can't rage about ghost projects while cutting corners at work. We can't expect systemic change while protecting our personal shortcuts.

Corruption isn't just a government problem. It's a people problem. It thrives because, in big and small ways, we allow it. We normalize it. We rationalize it. We pass it on to the next generation as just "how things are."

Real change starts when we stop treating corruption as a purely "them" issue. When we stop saying "Sila ang problema" and start asking "Ano ang ginagawa ko na nagpapalakas sa sistemang ito?"

I'm saying this to you, but I'm also saying it to myself. Because I know how easy it is to hide behind "content" and "advocacy" while my own habits remain untouched.

What if we actually paid the ticket instead of bribing the enforcer? What if we stopped voting for people who buy our votes? What if we called out friends and family when they participate in small corruptions instead of laughing it off?

Uncomfortable questions. But necessary ones.

Because we say we want a country where integrity matters. Where competence matters more than connections. Where our taxes go to real projects, not bank accounts.

That country will not fall from the sky. It will be built one choice at a time, by people who refuse to participate in the same corruption they complain about.

 The Mirror We Avoid

That flood control scandal showed us more than stolen billions. It showed how our values get twisted. How pakikisama becomes silence. How utang na loob becomes a leash. How integrity is redefined as loyalty to a person, not to what is right.

And it exposed us.

The politicians who stole. The contractors who paid. The bureaucrats who signed. The citizens who shrugged and said, "Ganyan talaga dito." We all had a role. We still do.

Every small corrupt act we commit gives permission for the bigger ones.

When we bribe an enforcer, we send the message that laws are optional. When we buy votes or sell ours, we turn democracy into a marketplace. When we lean on connections instead of fair process, we strengthen palakasan over justice.

We become the very system we curse.

And the worst part? We don't see it. We genuinely believe we're different from the politicians we criticize. We tell ourselves: "Ako, I'm just surviving. Sila, billions ang ninanakaw."

But the logic is the same. The justification is the same. The compromise is the same.

Only the scale is different.

 Questions Worth Sitting With

So maybe these are the questions worth keeping in our pocket:

Next time you share a post about corruption, ask: "Ano ang huling compromise na ginawa ko?"

Next time you rage about a politician's hypocrisy, ask: "Saan ako hipokrito sa sarili kong buhay?"

Next time you complain that nothing changes, ask: "Ano ang binago ko sa sarili ko?"

I'm asking these questions with you, not from a distance. I don't have a neat answer. What I have is discomfort, and maybe that's exactly where I need to stay for now.

This paradox won't fix itself. The gap between what we say and what we do won't close on its own. We have to close it.

The country we want will not be built by perfect people. It will be built by people who see their own complicity and choose differently anyway.

People who stop waiting for "sila" to change and start with "ako."

That's the only way out of this paradox.

The question is: handa ba talaga tayo? Ano sa palagay mo?

Your answer will reveal if Filipinos will continue on this path of corruption or not.

I didn’t know the details about the Venezuela bombing and the arrest of Nicolás Maduro. So, as usual, I did some reading. Let me give you a summary with a prayer that I didn't miss anything. 

 How things built up

For years, the United States and Maduro were already in conflict.

After Hugo Chávez died, Maduro took over a country with a broken economy and less and less space for opposition. Over time, critics were jailed or pushed into exile, and elections started to look less real.

In 2018, Venezuela held a presidential election that many observers and governments did not accept. Some opposition candidates were banned or abroad, and monitors said the vote was not free or fair. The US, the European Union, and several Latin American governments began calling Maduro’s rule illegitimate.

In 2019, the US and a group of allied countries went further. They recognized opposition leader Juan Guaidó as interim president of Venezuela, saying he was the rightful leader under their reading of the constitution. In practice, that meant the US stopped treating Maduro as a normal head of state.

At the same time, sanctions increased:

- Personal sanctions on Maduro’s allies and security officials 
- Measures against the central bank and the state oil company PDVSA 
- Limits on oil exports and access to the global financial system 

Officially, these were meant to pressure a corrupt and repressive government. In reality, the economic pain hit ordinary Venezuelans hardest.

 The criminal case against Maduro

While the political pressure grew, a criminal case was also being built in US courts.

In 2020, the US Department of Justice announced an indictment accusing Maduro and several senior officials of narco‑terrorism and large‑scale cocaine trafficking. The basic claim was:

- For more than 20 years, military and political figures in Venezuela formed a network often called the “Cartel of the Suns” inside the state 
- This network allegedly worked with armed groups like FARC and ELN in Colombia and with Mexican cartels to move tons of cocaine through Venezuela toward the US 
- Maduro was presented not as a bystander, but as one of the key figures behind this system 

In January 2026, a new, expanded indictment in New York added more detail. It:

- Linked Maduro to cooperation with FARC, ELN, the Sinaloa Cartel, the Zetas, and a Venezuelan gang called Tren de Aragua 
- Described how state resources like military airstrips and ports were used to ship cocaine 
- Named his wife Cilia Flores, his son “Nicolasito,” and ally Diosdado Cabello as part of the network 

Inside that US legal story, Maduro was no longer just an authoritarian leader. He was a wanted narco‑terrorist using his position as protection.

 Quiet military preparation

While this legal and political framing hardened, military moves were also happening.

By late 2025, the US had moved more forces into the Caribbean. There were warships, aircraft, and even a large aircraft carrier in the area. US forces increased actions against what they described as drug‑smuggling boats and also targeted oil shipments linked to Venezuela.

Publicly, this was explained as part of counter‑narcotics and sanctions enforcement. Looking back, it also looks like preparation for something bigger.

Analysts later reported that US planners considered several options:

- Limited actions at sea against drug traffickers 
- Limited airstrikes against air defenses and some military sites 
- Or a direct operation to capture Maduro on Venezuelan territory 

They chose the most aggressive option.

 The night of the strikes and capture

On the night of January 2–3, 2026, President Trump approved a mission called Operation Absolute Resolve.

What is known so far:

- More than 150 aircraft were involved, including fighters, bombers, drones, and refueling planes 
- The first wave of airstrikes hit Venezuelan air defense systems and selected military facilities, especially near the capital, Caracas 
- Under that cover, US special operations forces flew in low over the water toward Caracas by helicopter and tilt‑rotor aircraft 

Their target was the compound where US intelligence believed Maduro and Cilia Flores were staying.

Reports say there was gunfire and resistance from Venezuelan security forces. Some buildings in and around the compound were destroyed or damaged. No US deaths were reported and only a few US troops were injured.

At some point, Maduro and Flores tried to move to a safe room in the compound. US officials say they were intercepted and forced to surrender. By morning, Maduro was no longer in Caracas. He was in US custody, first on a ship, then flown to New York to face the expanded charges in court.

Soon after, Trump publicly said the US would “run” Venezuela for now and “get the oil flowing,” making it clear this was not just about drugs, but also about control and resources.

 Arrest or kidnapping?

This is the central question.

On US paper, it is an arrest. Under international law, many people see it as a kidnapping carried out with bombs and special forces.

From the US point of view:

- Maduro had been indicted in US federal court for narco‑terrorism and drug trafficking 
- Officials described him as a fugitive from US justice who hid behind a fraudulent presidency 
- They called the mission a law enforcement operation supported by the military, not a classic war or a simple regime‑change invasion 

Within that frame, they say it was an arrest: a wanted suspect, taken and brought before a court.

But if you look at it from the outside:

- The operation began with airstrikes on a sovereign country 
- Foreign special forces entered its capital city without permission 
- The de facto president was seized and flown out, without consent from Venezuelan authorities and without any mandate from the United Nations Security Council 

International law experts point to the UN Charter, which bans the use of force against the territorial integrity or political independence of another state, except in self‑defense or with UN approval. They also point to the usual rule that sitting heads of state have immunity from foreign criminal courts while they are in office.

From that point of view, a US indictment does not give legal permission to bomb another country and drag out its leader in handcuffs. That is why Venezuelan leaders and many foreign governments use the words “kidnapping” and “abduction” in their reactions.

So the most honest way to describe it is this:

- In US domestic legal language, it is presented as the arrest of an indicted narco‑terrorist 
- In the language of international law and state sovereignty, many jurists and governments describe it as an illegal use of force and an armed kidnapping of a foreign head of state 

Both descriptions exist at the same time. Which one someone chooses usually depends on where they stand and which rules they think should matter more.

My Meta Account Was Hacked—And My Panicked Fixes Made Everything Worse

On October 19th, I lost access to my Morning Coffee Thoughts page. It wasn't a forgotten password or a technical glitch. My Meta account had been hacked.

What started as a single compromised account turned into a nightmare I'm still dealing with. And the worst part? My desperate attempts to fix it alone made everything worse.

If you own a Facebook page, run Instagram, or manage Meta ads, this is important to you. Because Meta doesn't work in silos. Everything is connected. When one account gets compromised, they all do.

 How It Started: The Long Game

Meta accounts are all tied together. Facebook, Instagram, Meta Ads, Meta Business Suite—they're not separate systems. They're one interconnected beast.

My Instagram got hacked months ago. I didn't know it. The hackers got in and then just... waited. They were patient. They sat there dormant, doing nothing, just watching.

Then I linked that Instagram to my monetized Meta Business Suite account for Morning Coffee Thoughts.

That's when they moved.

The moment I made that connection, they activated. Suddenly they didn't just have Instagram—they had access to Facebook, Meta Ads, Meta Business Suite, and all the financial stuff connected to it. A dormant threat became an active one.

 My Stupid Mistakes: How I Made It Worse

I spent days getting nowhere with Meta Support. Four cases opened. Two were duplicates that they closed. One escalation went somewhere and then disappeared. Nothing was working.

So I did what any panicked person does. I tried to fix it myself.

Huge mistake.

Mistake #1: Thinking I Could Just Remove Them

I found out later that my access got downgraded to "partial access" while the hackers had full admin access. So when I tried to kick out the two unknown users from my Meta Business Suite, it didn't work. I couldn't remove them. Only they could remove me.

I didn't realize any of this while I was trying.

Mistake #2: Disconnecting Everything

I thought if I disconnected everything—Facebook from Instagram, Instagram from Meta Ads, everything from everything—I could contain it. Stop them from moving between accounts.

It seemed logical at the time. It wasn't.

When I finally got someone helpful on the phone weeks later, she couldn't put the pieces back together because I'd severed all the connections. The Meta Business Suite was locked. She had nothing to work with.

 What Actually Happened

I logged into my Meta Ads account and checked the People tab. Fifty accounts I'd never seen before were logged in. Fifty. All of them pushing freelancing and Upwork ads. They weren't just sitting there—they were actively using my account, my money, my credibility.

Four different people had access to my accounts. Four strangers in my business.

That's when I knew this was organized.

 Seven Weeks of Going Nowhere

Four cases. Two were duplicates. One had two phone calls and several chats. One agent seemed to actually care. Then they said they'd escalate it to another team. That team never called.

Here's what irritates me: during those first few cases, this could have been solved in November.

I was locked out, sure. But my bank account was still linked to the monetized page. Morning Coffee Thoughts had a depository account for payouts. Meta knew about it. All they had to do was verify that the bank account was still mine, ask me to prove it, and then help me reset. Simple. The kind of thing any bank does every day.

Not one agent thought to do that. They all just focused on account access—the thing the hackers controlled.

I reached out beyond the normal support channels too. Ms. Maoi, a friend and follower of MCT, knew someone who used to work at Facebook Philippines. She gave me the country manager's contact. I emailed them. Nothing. Morning Coffee Thoughts is small. To a country manager handling thousands of problems, I was nobody.

Nutribun Republic tried to help too. They sent emails on my behalf. But corporate doesn't always respond to individual people asking for help.

The whole thing felt broken.

 The Blue Badge I Dropped

My account used to be verified. It had the blue badge. Then I unenrolled to save money. It's a small recurring cost, but every dollar counts when you're running a page on your own.

When you have account verification, Meta takes you seriously. You get priority support. You get phone calls, not just scattered chats. They actually care about your case.

And here's something practical: if you don't respond to support messages, Meta closes your case. That sounds harsh until you understand why—they have turnover times, and if you're not engaged, they assume it's done. But verified accounts? They handle those differently. There's actual back-and-forth accountability.

I hate that I'm about to say this because I'm literally promoting Facebook here, but if you own a page? Get verified. RIGHT NOW. 

Yes, it costs money. But compare that to losing a monetized page. Compare it to the stress, the lost support you get from income, the not knowing if you'll ever get it back. That subscription fee is cheap insurance.

I re-enrolled in verification the second I realized I was hacked. Too late, but it's part of my case now. I should never have dropped it.

 Today's Call: Something Actually Happened

This morning I had a 33-minute call with someone from Meta Support who actually listened.

I walked her through everything. The hacking, the panicked disconnections, the failed escalations, trying to fix it myself and making it worse.

Halfway through I told her: "My attempts to fix this actually made it worse. I thought I was containing the damage, but I was destroying the only ways I had to get back in."

She got it. And then she did something the other agents didn't do. She made an actual promise. Not a vague "we'll escalate it." An actual commitment that she would personally watch my case and send me updates.

And she's doing it. The updates are coming.

 Where This Stands

My account isn't recovered yet. I still can't access Morning Coffee Thoughts. But I have something I didn't have this morning: someone actively working on it. Someone who promised they wouldn't disappear.

She said after we get the account back, there might be a way to fuse the two accounts. There's a qualifier—it's not guaranteed—and it could take a few weeks. But first things first. We get the account restored.

I'm waiting. Cautiously hopeful. But waiting.

 What I Know Now

If this happens to you, don't panic-fix it like I did. Don't try to remove users yourself. Don't disconnect things hoping to isolate the problem.

Call Meta Support. Stay with it. Open a case and then actually monitor the chat. Don't let it go silent or they'll close it. Be specific about what happened. Document everything. Screenshot everything (you will need it later when you talk to Support). 

If you still have access to your account, change your password first. Then contact support. Two-factor authentication on everything, right now, don't wait.

[Side note: use Google's Authenticator for 2FA. It's effective and very easy to set up.]

Keep your account verified (blue badge). It matters. It actually, genuinely matters.

Check who has access to your accounts. Go to the People tab. See who's logged in. Know what's happening in there.

And about Instagram—check it. Your Instagram might be compromised without you knowing it. Hackers wait for the moment when you connect it to something that matters. Something monetized. Then they move. Check your settings. Check your security. Don't assume it's fine.

 Still Waiting

I don't have a happy ending yet. But I have a path forward. I have someone who's actually paying attention.

To other page owners in the Philippines who've dealt with Meta's support system and felt like they were talking to a wall—I see you. If this happens to you, don't do what I did. Don't panic. Don't give up.

I'll update you when there's news. For now, I'm taking it one day at a time.

Have you been hacked? Are you dealing with Meta support right now? Put it in the comments. Let's see what's actually happening out there.
