This question was left in the comment section by one of our community members, Mr. Jose A. Carandang, and despite a jetlag‑induced migraine, it made me think:

Are we matured enough and ready for democracy?

I know how it sounds. Dangerous. Almost treasonous to some. But here I am, turning it over in my mind, because sometimes the questions we’re most afraid to ask are the ones that matter most.

It also makes me think about what Lee Kuan Yew said about us once. 

Back in 1992, speaking to a room full of business leaders in Manila, he said something that continues to echo in my head: “I do not believe that democracy necessarily leads to development. I believe that what a country needs to develop is discipline more than democracy.”

Harsh. Direct. And maddeningly difficult to dismiss.

Because when you look around, when you see how elections play out like telenovelas—all drama, betrayal, family feuds—you start to wonder if he had a point. 

When vote‑buying is so normalized that candidates budget for it like campaign materials. When the same families pass power from father to son to daughter to cousin, decade after decade, as if public office were inheritance rather than public trust.

When 80 percent of Congress comes from political dynasties.

When we pride ourselves on being “Asia’s oldest democracy” while most Filipinos say our democracy has serious problems.

 The soft, forgiving culture 

Lee Kuan Yew noticed something else about us, something that cut deeper. He said Filipinos have a “soft, forgiving” culture. He pointed out that only in the Philippines could someone like Ferdinand Marcos, who pillaged the country for over twenty years, still be considered for a national burial.

At the time, that must have seemed absurd to him.

Now? Marcos’ son is president.

Kasi we forgive too easily. We forget too quickly. Utang na loob binds us to our oppressors. Pakikisama keeps us silent when we should speak up. We mistake these values for virtues when sometimes they’re chains.

I’m not saying these cultural traits are automatically bad. 

Forgiveness can be grace. 
Loyalty can be strength. 

But in politics, in governance, they become weapons used against us. 

They keep corrupt officials in power because “kawawa naman,” because “he helped my tita once,” because “at least he’s better than the other guy.”

This is what Lee Kuan Yew once described as something missing in us, a kind of gel that holds society together. 

He observed that our elite mestizos treat ordinary Filipinos the way hacienda owners in Latin America treat their peons - a low-ranking worker such as an attendant, orderly, or assistant. 

You are looking at two different societies, one living in extreme luxury while the other scrapes by. 

And he was right. We have an oligarchy pretending to be a democracy.

The elite at the top are not interested in fixing the system because the system works perfectly for them.

 The American‑style trap 

Here’s where it gets complicated.

Lee Kuan Yew also criticized our presidential system, the American‑style constitution we adopted, with its strict separation of powers. 

He called it one of the most difficult to operate in the world, designed for a wealthy, underpopulated continent, not for a developing country struggling with disorder and underdevelopment.

And he had a point there too.

Our presidents serve six‑year terms but often spend the first two years figuring things out, the middle two actually governing, and the last two as lame ducks while everyone positions for the next election. 

Congress and the executive branch are often at war with each other, even when they’re from the same party, because we don’t have real political parties, just loose coalitions held together by patronage.

Presidential systems concentrate too much power in one person, inviting autocracy (a system of government by one person with absolute power). 

But they also create gridlock when different factions control different branches. It’s the worst of both worlds.

Parliamentary systems, like what Singapore has, fuse the executive and legislative branches. 

The majority party forms the government. 

If you lose the confidence of parliament, you’re out. It can be more stable, more accountable, and many studies say parliamentary systems tend to produce better economic outcomes and stronger rule of law.

But here’s the thing: would a parliamentary system actually fix our problems, or would it just make it easier for dynasties to consolidate power?

Because the problem isn’t just the system. It’s who’s running it.

 The Singapore temptation 

I know what some of you are thinking. So we need a Lee Kuan Yew. Someone strong. Someone who can impose discipline.

Tempting, isn’t it? Especially when you’re stuck in traffic for three hours because of flooding and bad infrastructure. 

When you see politicians more interested in TikTok fame than legislation. When corruption is so blatant it doesn’t even pretend to hide anymore.

Singapore did it. Authoritarian governance, strict controls, but also dramatic development. Clean streets. Functioning systems. A level of prosperity that makes ours look embarrassing.

But that came with a price.

It cost free speech. It cost a free press. It cost the ability to criticize the government without fear of lawsuits or worse. Lee Kuan Yew built Singapore by silencing dissent, jailing opposition leaders, making examples of anyone who challenged the ruling party.

In short, most of us Filipinos, the way we are today, will not survive the democracy the way Lee Kuan Yew envisioned and implemented it. 

[Side comment: Magbasa nga lang ng mahabang artikulo, di mo magawa, disiplina pa kaya? Nyeta.] 

And here’s the darker truth: authoritarian development models work best when you have a genuinely competent, genuinely visionary autocrat. Singapore got that in Lee Kuan Yew.

Who do we have?

We got Marcos. We got Duterte. We got leaders who use authoritarian tactics not to build the nation but to protect their own power and wealth.

That’s the gamble with authoritarianism. You might get Singapore. But you’re more likely to get what we already had, dictatorship without the development, repression without the prosperity.

 What maturity actually means 

So back to Jose’s question: Are we matured enough for democracy?

Maybe the question itself is off.

Democracy isn’t something you mature into like puberty. It’s not a medal you receive for good behavior. It’s a practice, a daily choice, a constant struggle.

The real question is this: are we willing to do the work that democracy requires?

Because democracy isn’t just voting every few years. 

It’s accountability. 
It’s transparency. 
It’s strong institutions that can check power. 
It’s political parties built on platforms instead of personalities. 
It’s a citizenry educated enough to see through populist tricks and brave enough to demand better.

We actually have some of the foundations. 

The 1987 Constitution, for all its flaws, established separation of powers, term limits, independent commissions, local autonomy, and protections for rights. It has survived coups, crises, and presidents who tried to bend it.

We have a highly visible and active civil society. NGOs, people’s organizations, church groups, sectoral groups. They have organized, protested, monitored elections, exposed scandals. 

Twice, people power has helped remove presidents from office.

We have grassroots initiatives that work. Community organizing. Attempts at participatory budgeting where communities help decide how public money gets spent. Local groups pushing for transparency at the barangay and city level.

But these efforts keep getting undermined. 

Programs that empower citizens are branded as political tools and shut down when a new administration feels threatened. 

Democratic spaces open under reform‑minded leaders, then shrink under those with authoritarian tendencies.

It’s exhausting. Two steps forward, three steps back.

 The path forward 

If I’m being honest, the answer is not choosing between democracy and something else. 

The answer is choosing better democracy.

That means passing an anti‑dynasty law that actually has teeth. Not the watered‑down promise politicians throw around during campaigns, but a real law with real limits on the concentration of power within families.

That means campaign finance reform so elections aren’t auctions where the highest‑spending clan wins.

That means strengthening political parties, requiring them to have real ideology, clear platforms, and internal accountability, instead of just being vehicles for whoever is popular at the moment.

That means investing in voter education, not just teaching people how to fill out a ballot, but how to evaluate information, spot lies, and think about long‑term consequences.

That means constitutional reforms, maybe even structural changes, but always with one goal: decentralize power, strengthen local governance, and build guardrails so no single leader or clan can hijack the system.

That means changing the culture, and this is the hardest part. 

Teaching the next generation that accountability matters more than loyalty. That principles matter more than personalities. That being kind and forgiving does not require tolerating impunity.

 Discipline within democracy 

Here’s where Lee Kuan Yew was right: we do need discipline. But discipline does not require authoritarianism.

Discipline means showing up to vote in every election, not just the big ones. 

Discipline means doing your homework on candidates instead of voting based on celebrity or last name. 

Discipline means holding officials accountable throughout their term, not just when campaign season starts again.

Discipline means demanding more from our leaders and from ourselves.

The Philippine problem is not that we have too much democracy. The problem is we have too little good governance, too little accountability, too many dynasties, and too few consequences for corruption.

We do not need less democracy. We need democracy that actually works for the majority, not just for the elite.

And that will not come from a strongman on a white horse. 

It will not come from simply switching to a parliamentary, federal, or any form of system while keeping the same political families. 

It will not come from trying to copy Singapore’s authoritarian model without Singapore’s competence and constraints.

It will come from us. 

From organizing. 
From refusing to sell our votes. 
From supporting candidates who have platforms instead of just slogans. 
From building institutions that are stronger than any individual leader.

Democracy is exhausting. It is messy. It will disappoint us again and again.

But when I look at people like Sir Jose, who ask uncomfortable questions instead of just scrolling past, I think: okay, there's still someone paying attention.

Are we mature enough for democracy?

I don't know. But I know what happens if we stop trying.
168:
2025 year felt like watching the same movie over and over, just with different actors and bigger budgets. 

Every month there was a new “crisis,” a new scandal, a new hearing. But when I zoom out, the lessons weren’t new at all. They were old truths made louder.

2025 didn’t just give headlines. It gave patterns.

Lesson 1: Accountability Is Usually "For Show Only"

On paper, 2025 looked like a year of “accountability.”

Rodrigo Duterte was arrested on an ICC warrant in March, the first Philippine president to face an international tribunal for drug war killings. 

Sara Duterte became the first vice president to be impeached, with over 200 House members signing a complaint that accused her of corruption, plotting to assassinate Marcos, and links to extrajudicial killings. 

Martin Romualdez resigned as Speaker after being dragged into the flood control scandal.

If you list all of that, it sounds like a system finally working.

But then you follow the endings.

The Supreme Court stopped Sara’s impeachment, calling it unconstitutional, and the Senate archived the case so she stayed in office. 

Duterte was in The Hague, but his allies dominated the May midterms and Bong Go topped the Senate race with a record vote count. 

The flood control scandal exposed how a huge chunk of a multibillion-peso program went to a small group of contractors, with ghost projects and substandard works, yet by year-end there were still no real consequences for the biggest names.

So the lesson for me was simple and ugly: our institutions are very good at acting like they’re fixing things, without actually changing who gets protected and who gets punished.

We saw hearings, reports, press cons, resignations “out of delicadeza.” We did not see the kind of punishment that makes other officials think twice.

Lesson 2: Some Politicians Can Be Cornered and Still Winning

Duterte being arrested should have been the ultimate downfall.

Instead, it turned him into something else in the public conversation.

While he was facing charges for crimes against humanity, his political network stayed alive and aggressive. 

In May, his camp still secured seats in the Senate, with Bong Go landing in the top spot. 

Duterte remained central to local politics, especially in Davao, and his name still carried weight in national debates.

That tells me this: legal cases and influence don’t move together.

You can be on trial abroad and still shape what happens here. 

You can be physically absent but still very present in who gets elected, what narratives spread, and which policies move forward.

If I’m honest, that scares me more than the arrest comforts me.

Lesson 3: We Say We’re Tired of Dynasties, Then Still Vote for Them

The 2025 midterm elections were loud, emotional, and very familiar.

Turnout was high. People lined up. People argued online. 

People said they wanted change. But if you look at who actually won, political families still dominated almost every level.

Many provinces stayed under “fat dynasties,” where one family holds multiple seats at the same time. 

Analysts pointed out that dynasties weren’t just surviving, they were tightening their grip. 

The fallout between Marcos and Duterte didn’t weaken the system of political families, it just turned into a turf war over who controls which part.

The painful lesson here: we are not just victims of dynasties, we’re also part of why they keep winning.

Not because people are stupid, but because the setup is rigged in their favor. 

They have money, machinery, and name recall. 

Opponents usually have none of that. So you can genuinely want something different, but when you’re faced with a ballot full of familiar surnames and weak alternatives, the old names win again.

Lesson 4: The Streets Can Make Noise, But They Can’t Finish the Job Alone

The Trillion Peso March stood out for me.

Thousands went to the streets to protest corruption tied to flood control money. 

More actions followed. Different groups showed up: church people, students, workers, professionals. 

The message was clear: we are tired of being robbed while communities flood and budgets quietly disappear.

At Mendiola, things turned ugly. People were injured. Some died. Hundreds were arrested. 

The government framed parts of it as a “paid riot” or an organized attack. 

Protesters insisted it was anger spilling over from years of being ignored and cheated.

Did the protests matter? Yes. They forced the issue to stay in the headlines. They pressured officials to at least act like they were doing something. They reminded those in office that people are watching.

Did they change who really gets their way in this country? Not yet.

And that’s the lesson that hurts the most: protest is necessary, but it’s not enough by itself. 

The streets can open a door, but if there’s no follow-through in organizing, in elections, in legal work, the door quietly closes and the system goes back to normal.

Lesson 5: Our “Safety Nets” Have Big Holes

The Alice Guo story felt unreal, but it was very real.

A local official tied to POGO hubs, trafficking, and alleged foreign interference gets exposed, tried, and sentenced. 

Her case revealed how she managed to run, win, and govern despite basic questions about her identity and operations.

That’s more than just “isang mayor na abusado.” 

It shows how easy it is to slip through the cracks if you have money, backers, and the right paperwork. 

It says something about our vetting, our background checks, our national security awareness, and how much we rely on trust instead of verification.

Same story with the flood control mess. Billions were allocated. Some projects were ghosts. Some were basura quality. 

A few names only surfaced after journalists, auditors, and citizens started asking noisy questions.

So the lesson isn’t just “we have corruption.” It’s “our safeguards don’t work the way we think they do.” 

The gates that are supposed to protect us are weak, slow, or easily bypassed.

Lesson 6: All This Drama Shows Up in the Economy

None of these political stories stay in the abstract. They turn into something you can feel.

By late 2025, growth slowed down. 

Public construction took a hit, partly because projects were caught in budget fights and corruption probes. Investors became more cautious. 

Business groups started saying the same thing: if the mess continues, the next years will be rough.

All the hearings, scandals, and pa-imbestiga don’t just live on TV. They affect jobs, prices, and basic stability.

Corruption looks abstract when you only talk about stolen amounts.

But it shows up in real life as floods because drainage projects were never done properly.

It shows up as delayed roads and bridges that could have made daily life easier.

It shows up as overcrowded classrooms and thin school resources.

It shows up in that quiet frustration that no matter how hard people work, the system always seems tilted against them.

That’s the real cost.

Lesson 7: Family Drama at the Top Is a Distraction and a Warning

The tension between the Marcos and Duterte camps colored a lot of 2025.

You had rallies calling for peace between them. 

You had speeches, shade, pointed remarks. 

You had Cabinet officials resigning or being replaced. 

You had agencies shifting hands, and people trying to read between the lines every time someone was moved or “reassigned.”

On the surface, it looked like pure personality drama. Underneath, it was about who controls the budget, who leads which institution, whose people sit in key positions.

The lesson I took from that: when two big families fight, the whole government feels it. 

Policies shift not because of evidence or public need, but because of internal calculations. Appointments become part of the tug-of-war.

The rule doesn’t really change: families first, country second. 

And unless that changes at the ballot box, we’ll see the same story again in 2028, just with a slightly different script.

When I put all these lessons together, they boil down to this:

The Philippines in 2025 reminded me that our politics is very good at noise and very weak at closure. 

We know how to expose. We rarely know how to finish. 

We can be brave in the streets and still stuck in the same patterns during elections. 

Our laws and systems look strong on paper, but in real life they bend depending on who’s involved.

It’s frustrating. Nakakapagod.

But seeing the pattern clearly still matters to me. 

The moment you stop treating every scandal as something “shocking” and start seeing it as part of a routine, you also start seeing where you need to focus: which hearings matter, which budgets to watch, which names keep showing up, and which choices in 2028 will either repeat the same story or finally interrupt it.

What has been released so far from the Leviste Files

[Warning: Long piece ahead.]

Leviste calls them the Cabral files.

Government says they are unauthenticated. The Ombudsman says they are incomplete. DPWH says they might be stolen.

For this piece, they are the Leviste Files: a political bomb made of documents only he has fully seen, arranged the way only he wants them told.

And just because I am writing about them does not mean everything in them is true.

These files are powerful, but they are also fragile: editable spreadsheets, forwarded copies, screenshots, PDFs that could have been altered. The originals are with government; what we see are his versions.

So my curiosity here is simple: ano nga ba talaga ang laman ng mga ito, according to him?

At best, they are clues. They might be accurate, they might be incomplete, and they are definitely soaked in politics.

 What the Leviste Files claim to show

At the heart of the Leviste Files is a simple, brutal message: hindi pantay ang laro, at hindi pantay ang pondo.

The picture he paints of the DPWH budget from 2023 to 2026 is around P3.5 trillion in total. That is life-and-death money for roads, bridges, flood control, or, if abused, kickbacks, ghost projects, and patronage.

Region by region, it already feels lopsided. Central Luzon gets the biggest slice. Calabarzon follows. Bicol and NCR are high. At the bottom, BARMM gets the least.

You look at those numbers and you start to see what “priority” really means in this government. Some regions get a firehose. Others, a dripping faucet.

Pero hindi lang ito tungkol sa geography. The Leviste Files are less about where, and more about who.

 Allocable, outside allocable, and the formula

This is the technical part, but this is where the heat is.

He says the files reveal a system with two kinds of money:

- Allocable or “NEP restored” funds that district reps can supposedly touch, adjust, or reassign 

- Outside allocable funds controlled by higher-ups, inserted into districts without the local congressman’s consent or full knowledge 

Put together, we are talking about hundreds of billions in play every year.

The architecture of this system is linked to the late DPWH undersecretary Maria Catalina “Cathy” Cabral, who reportedly built a parametric formula to determine how much each district gets. 

Inside DPWH, the impression was that she was the only one who truly understood the formula.

If that is true, then one person held the keys to trillions.

That is the first unsettling pattern the Leviste Files force us to confront: the budget comes with a power structure disguised as math.

And we have to keep in mind: what we are looking at are his copies.

Investigators are still comparing those against official databases. For now, every table is a claim, not a verdict.

 Sandro, Romualdez, and the billion-peso gap

Then we get to the names.

The documents he has released, along with independent reporting, draw a picture like this: the president’s son, Sandro Marcos, and the president’s cousin, Martin Romualdez, sit at the top of the allocation ladder, with multi-year totals in the tens of billions, while most district representatives are somewhere far lower, in the single-digit billions.

Every congressman, he claims, gets at least a basic allocation in the hundreds of millions. But some get far, far more.

Everyone gets something, but some get almost everything.

That part is not illegal on its face. 

Congress can argue that politics and power always shape budgets. 

But politically and ethically, it smells like old pork in new packaging: the family and inner circle get the choicest cuts, everyone else is reminded to be grateful for scraps.

Since the spreadsheets have not yet survived full forensic checking, the most honest posture is this: the pattern looks very familiar, but the final numbers are still a working draft, not a certified ledger.

 The P8 billion ghost projects

Then comes the thriller twist.

On New Year’s Eve, he released another batch: 75 DPWH projects worth around P8 billion, stamped with a strange label: “OP (ES/SAP).”

That is Office of the President, Executive Secretary, Special Assistant to the President. Or at least that is how the acronym reads.

The list is full of flood control and streetlight projects scattered across the country.

The standout example is a solar streetlight project on the Tagaytay–Nasugbu Highway, inside his own Batangas district. 

Hundreds of millions of pesos for a little over a thousand units, ending with a per-unit price so high it looks obscene beside normal market estimates. 

He also flags insanely expensive “cat’s eye” road reflectors.

When he asked DPWH who “OP (ES/SAP)” actually was, no one could give a straight answer. When the Executive Secretary’s office was asked, they denied being proponents.

So we are left with P8 billion tied to a label everyone recognizes but nobody claims.

So, tanungin mo ang sarili mo: sloppy accounting ba ito, or sinasadyang guluhin ang linya ng pananagutan?

Complicating things, Palace officials are already saying that some circulating lists do not match official records, that there are conflicting versions, some with handwritten notes, some naming Cabinet members and some not. 

That does not erase the suspicion, but it reminds us we are dealing with contested documents that may have changed hands, and changed shape, multiple times.

 The P2 million Christmas money

As if that were not enough, he talked about another practice: colleagues allegedly receiving P2 million checks around Christmas, booked as MOOE, or maintenance and operating expenses.

No receipts. No detailed liquidation. Just certifications that the money went to office expenses, fuel, representation, and the like.

House leaders pushed back fast. They insisted it is not a Christmas bonus but a regular MOOE release. 

They called the word “bonus” unfair and misleading. 

Makabayan lawmakers publicly said they did not receive any such P2 million.

He, meanwhile, says some colleagues told him not to post photos of the checks, not to talk openly about it, not to blow up an internal arrangement into a public issue.

On paper, this setup can be technically legal. In practice, if there are no receipts and no real transparency, it functions like a quiet seasonal payout.

That is the pattern again: the law is the costume, the abuse is the body underneath.

And yes, agencies now say that the P2 million released to him had no irregularity based on their initial checks. That is exactly why readers have to hold both things at once: the system can be legal and still be corrupt.

Question is, can you hold two opposing ideas in your head and still remain analytical?

 Where the files really came from

Now we reach the muddiest part: the origin story.

His story is this: on a September day in 2024, Undersecretary Cabral personally handed him the files at DPWH, with the knowledge and blessing of DPWH Secretary Vince Dizon. She did this, he says, to make sure the documents would not vanish and to promote transparency.

Dizon’s story is the exact opposite. He denies giving any such order. He says the files were forcibly accessed or taken from Cabral’s staff, without consent. DPWH staff back him, claiming there was chaos in the office and that Cabral herself ended up with paper cuts struggling with documents.

He says CCTV footage shows a calm, voluntary turnover and that the “paper cut” story is part of a cover-up.

The Ombudsman adds another wrinkle: when he met them, they say he did not turn over the full set of files he keeps talking about, only limited portions. They now have Cabral’s official computer and are doing forensic work to see what really existed on her end, independent of him.

Cabral cannot speak.

In December 2025, she fell into a ravine in Benguet and died. Police and forensic teams say all the scans and reconstructive tests point to a fall or slide, not a push. The Justice Secretary echoed that conclusion publicly.

The timing, though—she dies, then days later he starts dropping files—makes it impossible for people not to speculate.

This is the part where caution becomes crucial. Because until the Ombudsman finishes checking the original hardware and official records, what we have are conflicting claims and third-party copies. Powerful, yes. But fragile.

 The system fights back

Once the files hit the public, the backlash came quickly.

The Palace said the documents cannot be treated as conclusive evidence and passed the burden of authentication to the Ombudsman. 

They pointed out that many different versions are circulating and that stories built on unverified files will remain speculation until the Ombudsman speaks.

The Ombudsman, for its part, emphasized that documents held by private individuals, especially soft copies that can be edited, are weak as evidence until matched against official records. 

They stressed that their work will rely on original government files, not social media posts.

Inside Congress, House public accounts chair Terry Ridon is openly floating an ethics complaint—not on the numbers, but on how the files were allegedly obtained. 

His response: he would “enjoy” an ethics case because it would give him a platform to talk about the budget on record.

So while the public is asking where the money went, the political class is more focused on who grabbed which files and how.

It is the classic move: attack the procedure, dodge the substance.

 What the Leviste Files really are

Strip away the noise, and the Leviste Files are:

- A partial, contested set of budget documents linked to Cathy Cabral, filtered and released by one congressman 

- A window into a DPWH system where allocable and outside allocable funds give higher-ups huge leverage over legislators 

- A rough X-ray of how the president’s son and cousin ended up with far larger infrastructure allocations than ordinary districts 

- A trail of questionable projects, from overpriced solar streetlights to P8 billion in ghost-branded “OP (ES/SAP)” flood controls 

- A set of clues about quiet practices like the P2 million Christmas MOOE releases—technically defensible, but ethically ugly 

They are not court-proven. 
They are not Ombudsman-certified. 
They are not yet historical fact.

They are politically real, but forensically unproven.

That is why people need to treat them as serious but unfinished: potentially explosive, but also potentially tainted. Moving outside official chains of custody made the files more visible, but also made them easier to question.

 Why this feels different

Part of why the Leviste Files feels strange is the messenger.

He is not the usual whistleblower archetype. He is not a poor bureaucrat risking everything. He is a rich kid, a neophyte, the son of a senator. Someone the system usually shelters, not targets.

And yet he is the one saying:

Every congressman gets a list of projects they can and cannot touch. 

The budget is padded with insertions even at the NEP stage, long before the public hears anything. 

Some districts are treated like personal fiefdoms, especially when your last name is the same as the president’s or the former Speaker’s.

When someone inside the club starts describing the machinery out loud, it becomes harder for everyone else to pretend they are just passengers.

 Eyes wide open

So how should people read what has been released so far from the Leviste Files?

Not as final verdict, but as a set of leads. 
Not as pure truth, but as political evidence that still needs technical verification. 
Not as something to worship, but not something to dismiss just because Malacañang says so.

You hold two thoughts at once:

The pattern exposed here—lopsided allocations, ghost proponents, quiet payouts—fits what many already suspect about the system. 

At the same time, the specific documents and numbers might be flawed, incomplete, or selectively presented to hit certain targets.

That is why the right attitude is open-eyed but not starstruck. Curious, but not gullible. Skeptical, but not numb.

The deeper question is not just whether the Leviste Files are authentic.

The deeper question is: now that we have seen how the game might be played, what do we do with that knowledge, knowing the data might be messy, incomplete, and possibly tainted?

Do we file it under tsismis and move on? Or do we start asking our own congressman very simple questions: 

- How much DPWH money did our district get from 2023 to 2026? 
- How much of that did you actually control? - Are there projects in our area you did not propose but are booked under your district?
- Did you receive a P2 million MOOE release in December, and can you show how you spent it?

If they cannot answer clearly, then what has already been released from the Leviste Files has done part of its job, even before the Ombudsman finishes its work.

Because my point here is never just to win a case in court. 

The point I'm trying to make is to expose the pattern—and to force us to look, eyes wide open, and finally, to ask better questions.

Philippine Politics in 2026: One Citizen’s Take

[An MCT Commentary]

I’m finally back home and because I didn’t want to sleep yet, attempting to restore my messed up body clock, I decided to review my notes for 2025 and write this piece.

We’re coming from a 2025 na puro sabog: 

- the UniTeam finally broke beyond repair, 

- Rodrigo Duterte was arrested on an ICC warrant and flown to The Hague, 

- Sara Duterte went through an impeachment process that the Supreme Court later voided, 

- the flood control scandal shook the system, and Marcos’ numbers slid hard in the surveys. 

Hindi ito expert analysis or consultant-level forecast. 

This is just one citizen’s take, from someone who’s been watching, writing, and worrying about where our politics is heading.

 Marcos in 2026: Surviving, Not Thriving

By the end of 2025, Marcos walks into 2026 weaker than at any point in his presidency.

Survey numbers show his approval sliding into the low 30s, with almost half of respondents expressing disapproval or distrust, and particularly harsh ratings in Visayas and Mindanao. 

For a sitting president na hindi pa tapos ang term, mabigat yun.

A big driver of that anger is the flood control projects scandal.

Reports say around trillions have been poured into flood control in the last decade and a half, with a massive share suspected to have been lost to corruption. 

In his 2025 SONA, Marcos himself pointed out that about 100 billion pesos, roughly a fifth of his administration’s flood control spending, went to just 15 contractors, many linked to lawmakers and local officials.

Details that came out through media, leaks, and investigations include:

- Ghost projects and substandard flood defenses that failed as soon as typhoons hit 

- Insiders and contractors allegedly working together to inflate costs or fund non-existent works 

- Cash deliveries tied to powerful political figures, including then-Speaker Martin Romualdez and other legislators, mapped out in timelines and exposés

Government responded by forming a special commission, freezing some assets, and issuing lookout bulletins for certain contractors and personalities. 

Environmental and advocacy groups called it an obscene plunder of climate and disaster funds, since these projects were supposed to protect communities from floods.

On the streets, the Trillion Peso March turned frustration into bodies and banners. 

Massive rallies were held on dates heavy with symbolism, like September 21 and November 30, with people converging in Luneta, EDSA, and key provincial centers. 

Church groups, students, labor, professionals, and NGOs stood side by side, demanding punishment for the corrupt, recovery of stolen funds, stronger anti-corruption institutions, and serious political reform. 

Organizers were careful to keep it within constitutional lines, rejecting coup talk and calls for a military withdrawal of support.

Despite all this, by yearend only a few smaller fish and some poster contractors seemed genuinely at risk. The biggest names denied, lawyered up, or stayed very quiet.

Ang basa ko dito: Marcos tried to own the anti-corruption storyline by exposing the scandal, but he could not match the attempt with consequences. And Filipinos notice that gap.

On paper, 2026 is framed as part of economic recovery. In practice, the outlook is sobering. 

International lenders cut Philippine growth forecasts for 2025 and 2026, warning about slower expansion because of global factors, inflation, and domestic uncertainty. 

Projections land somewhere in the mid-single digits, which sounds fine for briefings, but does not guarantee better wages or lower prices.

Business groups keep calling 2026 a crucial year for reforms: cleaning up corruption, fixing infrastructure implementation, and tackling long-term problems like weak productivity and complicated rules. 

Congress ratified a record-high 2026 national budget, but critics point to questionable allocations, unprogrammed funds, and pork-like items hiding in technical language.

What this tells me: Marcos spends 2026 trying to prevent economic frustration from fully merging with political anger. 

The statistics may avoid full-blown crisis, but trust is already eroded. Without visible justice on corruption, even good policies will feel hollow.

 The Duterte Camp: Bruised, Not Broken

While Marcos bleeds politically, the Duterte camp remains bruised, but very far from finished.

In March 2025, Rodrigo Duterte was arrested on an international warrant and transferred to The Hague, facing charges tied to the drug war. 

For human rights advocates, it felt like a long-delayed step toward accountability. 

For hardcore supporters, it was confirmation na ginigiba ang idol nila by foreign and local enemies.

Even in detention, his influence did not disappear. 

He won the mayoralty in Davao City in 2025, showing how deep his roots still are in his home base. 

The case abroad will move slowly, with motions, hearings, and political drama attached to every development.

Sara Duterte, meanwhile, had her own political crucifixion. 

The rift between the Marcos and Duterte camps turned from quiet tension into open feud. 

She broke away from the UniTeam, clashed with Martin Romualdez, and her camp was accused of plotting against the president’s inner circle. 

The House impeached her over confidential and intelligence funds, leading to a dramatic Senate trial that was eventually cut short when the Supreme Court voided the impeachment on procedural grounds.

Despite everything, her ratings stayed stronger than Marcos’. 

Nationally, she held relatively high approval and trust scores, and in Mindanao, her numbers were overwhelming while Marcos’ were dismal. 

Early 2028 preference polls consistently place her in the top slot, often with support in the low to mid 30s, while other figures trail far behind.

In the 2025 midterms, Duterte-aligned candidates performed better than many expected. 

Allies kept key positions in Mindanao and beyond. Pro-Duterte names in the Senate race remained loud and relevant. 

The message on the ground is clear: the Duterte brand is not just alive, it is organized.

Sa akin, parang ganito ang snapshot: even with an arrested patriarch and an almost-removed vice president, the Duterte machinery and narrative still have serious reach. 

The danger for them is not vanishing support, but accumulating baggage: corruption questions, foreign policy doubts, and the constant shadow of human rights cases.

 Opposition And The Elusive Alternative

On paper, the opposition had some wins in 2025. In practice, it is still in the “sino ba talaga tayo” phase.

Analyses of the 2025 midterms highlight three things: 

- Marcos’ camp underperformed in the Senate,

- the Duterte bloc showed it could still swing votes, 

- reformist or opposition candidates scored meaningful gains in some local and party-list races. 

Voters sent a mixed message: pagod sa status quo, pero hindi pa buo ang tiwala sa alternatives.

Leni Robredo’s landslide win as mayor of Naga City became a symbol of localized hope. 

She launched a development roadmap for the city and quietly rebuilt from the ground up. 

Later on, her camp signaled she is unlikely to run for president in 2028, choosing to focus on Naga and her advocacies. 

That takes one big name out of the national equation, at least for now.

At the same time, there is growing talk of a progressive or democratic third force: a loose coalition of liberals, social democrats, youth groups, church networks, and civic organizations drawn together by issues like corruption, human rights, education, and dynasties. 

The Trillion Peso March became a key test space for this: if they can coordinate on the streets, can they coordinate at the ballot box?

Names floated for 2028 from this side include Risa Hontiveros, Bam Aquino, and some younger local executives and advocates whose public profiles rose in 2025. 

But everything is couched in maybe, if unity happens, if surveys improve. Walang solid anchor pa.

The pattern I see: 2026 is their organizing exam. 

If they stay as scattered brands and small parties, they will be drowned out by Duterte machinery and whatever is left of the Marcos coalition. 

If they manage to agree on a shared banner and minimum agenda, they become a real alternative for voters who are sick of dynastic ping-pong.

 Trillion Peso March And The 2026 Mood

The Trillion Peso March is not just a one-off protest. It is a mood that might carry into 2026.

It tied together multiple frustrations:

- Corruption in flood control and other infrastructure 

- Climate disasters made worse by missing or substandard projects 

- Dynasties and their grip on budgets 

- A feeling that government is captured by syndicates, not public servants

You had priests and pastors on stage, students on the streets, middle-class professionals carrying witty but angry placards, and workers marching shoulder to shoulder. 

The organizing language was anti-corruption, anti-dynasty, pro-accountability. 

Calls for military intervention were explicitly rejected, which is important in a country with a history of coups and people power.

The way it lands on me: people are trying to push the system to clean itself up without breaking it. 

That is a very Filipino compromise instinct. It is also very fragile. 

If 2026 brings more scandals with no consequences, expect bigger, angrier, and more frequent mobilizations.

 2026 As The Quiet Start Of 2028

The way I see it, 2026 is already part of the 2028 presidential race.

Early preference surveys and political columns line up Sara Duterte as the frontrunner, with other names scattered far behind. 

Some potential candidates will fade, others will suddenly appear, but the basic framing is there: Duterte versus whoever dares to challenge her, with Marcos trying not to look irrelevant, and the opposition trying not to implode.

In that context:

- Marcos will spend 2026 trying to avoid becoming a lame duck, even as his scandals and low ratings weigh down his endorsements. 

- Sara will keep sharpening the story that everyone is out to destroy her because she is strong, using impeachment, her father’s case, and attacks from Manila elites as proof. 

- The opposition will either do the slow, tiring work of unifying, or remain background noise in another season of dynastic drama. 

- Corruption, the ICC, and economic frustration will quietly color how people judge all of them, even when campaign language is full of unity, discipline, or strong leadership.

For people like us, ordinary citizens and pro good governance writers, 2026 will not give a neat resolution. 

Pero it will reveal character. 

Who protects their own at all costs. 

Who throws allies under the bus to save themselves. 

Who actually listens when people march, and who hides behind statements and spokespeople.

That is where this take really lives this year: not in grand predictions, but in watching who chooses what, when it finally matters.

171:
₱1.5 million break bonus daw.

What does that even mean? And why are we all acting like this is fine?

1. Let’s start with the ₱1.5M

Batangas Rep. Leandro Leviste says that on the day the Lower House was supposed to vote on the 2026 budget in October 2025, 1.5 million pesos was being given out to lawmakers.  

Same day as a crucial budget vote, same place, and money is moving quietly through the building for congressmen.

He also says lawmakers do not just get regular monthly funds. There are extra amounts in October and December, conveniently timed with budget approval moments.  

So that 1.5 million pesos people are talking about is one of those “break” payouts during the Undas or budget period, separate from the later December “Christmas bonus” talk.

And the more you sit with that, the dirtier it feels.

2. Then Toby Tiangco drops the real bomb

Navotas Rep. Toby Tiangco comes in and basically says: yes, this happens, and it has been happening for years.

He admits that every time the Lower House goes on break, lawmakers receive what he calls a break bonus or allowance.  

He even says this has been going on since at least 2010, when he first became a congressman. This is not a new thing under the current administration. This is standard Lower House behavior.

He explains the pattern like this:

- Lawmakers get monthly MOOE, the usual office funds.  

- On top of that, they get extra money when the House goes on recess: Undas, Christmas, Holy Week, and other breaks.  

- He openly confirms he received 1.5 million pesos for the Undas break.

And the process? They sign a voucher, they get the checks, and these particular break amounts do not require detailed liquidation.

That is not an accident. That is a system designed to move money without real scrutiny.

3. Then there’s the 2 million “not-a-bonus” bonus

Leviste also talks about another amount: around 2 million pesos per lawmaker in December, after the bicam report on the 2026 budget was ratified. He calls it a Christmas bonus for some House members.

The response from House leaders is almost insulting:

- They say this is not a Christmas bonus, but MOOE, office money, standard allocation.  

- They insist the funds are for district activities, Christmas events, consultations, and programs.  

- Others claim the checks seen in photos are meant for staff salaries and operations, not a bonus for lawmakers.

So they play this word game:

- 1.5 million in October? Break allowance.  

- 2 million in December? Office funds only, definitely not a bonus.  

- Yet both are sent in checks, both are handled inside the same opaque internal budget, and both sit on top of an already bloated MOOE.

They keep insisting it is legal. Maybe it is. That is the point. They turned legal structures into tools to quietly reward themselves.

4. This is what “legal corruption” looks like

Look at the pattern and tell me this is not theft with paperwork.

- The Lower House’s internal budget jumps by 10.5 billion pesos, up to 27.7 billion, with no clear public explanation.  

- MOOE alone hits 18.58 billion, with a huge chunk listed as “other maintenance and operating expenses,” vague and loaded, often liquidated by certification instead of real receipts.  

- Extra money drops in October and December, in the middle of and right after major budget decisions.  

- A staffer even shows Leviste a 151 million peso package and calls it his incentive before a vote.

They are not just breaking rules. They are writing the rules in a way that lets them grab more while still being able to say, “It’s allowed. It’s audited. It’s office funds.”

That is why it stings: they can say it is legal while everyone can see it is abusive.  
It is legal corruption, dressed up as MOOE, liquidations by certification, and break allowances.

And the worst part? They know most people will shrug and move on.

5. The part that really makes me angry

Tiangco saying this has been happening since 2010 is supposed to calm us down, like: relax, this is normal, this is how the Lower House works.  
For me, that makes it worse.

Because if this has been the habit for more than a decade, that means:

- We have been funding these break bonuses for years without being told they exist.  

- They are so used to it that they talk about 1.5 million and 2 million like it is just another day at work.  

- We are now at a point where they can openly admit it on TV and still expect nothing to change.

They keep finding new labels, new excuses, new categories in the budget. Office funds. Programs. For the district.  
We keep paying for it through taxes, higher prices, and broken services.

And most of us are so numb we are not even angry anymore. We just swallow it.  

We share a link. 
We rant for one night. 
Then we move on.

Meanwhile, they stay. They sign another voucher. Another check. Another break. Another not-a-bonus.

All legal.

And now, after staring at this mess in the Lower House, there is one more question I cannot shake:

If this is what is happening here,  
what does it look like on the Senate side?

The other question: how come I am hearing about this from Tiangco and Leviste, congressmen I gave zero trust to?

How come I am not hearing about it, loudly and clearly, from the progressive reformists in the Lower House — the Makabayan bloc, Gabriela, ACT Teachers, Kabataan, people like France Castro, Arlene Brosas, and Raoul Manuel?
172:
This comment was left earlier under my blog about Leandro Leviste. 

The wording alone made my ears ring. 

I want to give this comment the focus it deserves because when you multiply this kind of mindset by millions, you start to see why the Philippines is in real deep shit. 

"At least he spoke and opened about corruption." 

On the surface, it sounds harmless. Supportive pa nga. 

But this is exactly the mindset that keeps corruption alive in the Philippines: contento na sa bare minimum, then shocked when politicians treat us like basura. 

This is the same spirit as pwede na yan. Pwede na ang speech. Pwede na ang pa-strong words. Pwede na ang “may guts.” Even if walang track record, walang policy, walang malinaw na plano. 

 How This Mindset Feeds Corruption 

Corruption in the Philippines survives not only because of powerful people, but because ordinary citizens keep excusing them. 

When we say “at least he spoke,” we reward words, not work. 

When we say “bata pa siya, may guts,” we treat age and attitude as qualification, instead of competence and integrity. 

When we make excuses for politicians we like, we send the message that they can abuse power and still keep our support. 

Look at what happened under Rodrigo Duterte. 

Despite killings, controversies, and scandals, he stayed popular because many framed him as “at least may political will,” “at least anti-drugs,” as if toughness cancels out abuse and impunity. 

Now look at Sara Duterte. 

She faces serious questions about how public funds were used in her offices, yet some still say, “at least matapang,” “at least nagpalabas ng statement,” as if PR and legal defense equal innocence. 

Same formula. Different Duterte.

 Low Standards, Low-Quality Politicians 

When you tolerate low standards, you attract low-quality leaders. 

Cavite Rep. Kiko Barzaga is a good example. 

Instead of modeling maturity, he became known for reckless social media behavior and had to face House sanctions for it. 

This is an elected official, handling laws, budgets, and public trust, yet acting like a clout-chasing influencer. 

If your reaction is “at least he speaks against Marcos” or “at least he fights,” you are proving to politicians that they can be irresponsible, sexist, or dishonest, and still keep fans as long as they push the right emotional buttons. 

They learn fast. 

We teach them what they can get away with. 

 Raising The Bar, Not Just Raising Our Voice 

Here’s the standard, at least on my page: 

Speaking about corruption is the starting line, not the finish line. 

You don’t get extra credit for doing what is basic moral duty. 

“Guts” without competence, ethics, and consistency is just drama. 

If a young politician speaks against corruption, good. 

Question them pa rin: 

Ano na ang batas na na-file? 
Ano ang risk na tinanggap niya? 
Sino ang hinabla? 
Ano ang nabago sa sistema? 

If the answer is “wala pa, but may potential,” then treat him as what he is: a work in progress, not a hero. 

In fact, never treat any politician as a hero. Ever. 

 This Page Is a Coaching Zone

So yes, when you drop comments like “at least he spoke” on my posts, I will treat them as raw material for a bigger conversation. 

When you comment something like that on any of my posts, expect that when I see it, I will turn it into a coaching opportunity, a lesson to learn. 

Not to embarrass you. 
Not to flex being “woke.” 

But because this is how we unlearn pwede na yan thinking. 

Because every time we let that mindset pass, we help create the next Duterte, the next Barzaga, the next trapo who thinks Filipinos will always settle for scraps. 

If you stay on this page, expect this: 

Your comments are welcome. Your opinions are welcome. 

But your standards will be challenged.
173:
Rizal was right about the youth being the hope of the nation. But he never said all youth, all the time, no matter what they do. 

 When “pag-asa ng bayan” looks like clout 

There are days when that Rizal quote feels like an emotional scam. 

You grow up hearing “ang kabataan ang pag-asa ng bayan,” then you open your feed and see a congressman meowing in sessions, livestreaming his feelings, and getting suspended by the House ethics committee for reckless posts that supposedly harm the integrity of the institution. 

Yes, that is also “kabataan.” 

You see a young lawmaker turning his quirky “meow” persona into a brand of Gen Z politics, using memes and livestreams to rail against corruption and even call for the president’s resignation. 

On paper, that sounds brave. In practice, the line between courage and spectacle gets very thin when your politics depends on virality. 

So you sit there thinking: if this is the sample size of kabataan, tangina, talo ang Pilipinas.

 The Leviste kind of “pag-asa” 

Then there is the other type. 

Leandro Leviste, batang congressman, business background, talks about reducing political positions through an “anti-politician bill” and brands himself as someone willing to shrink the political class. 

He made headlines exposing alleged bribery in flood control projects, filing cases after an entrapment operation over millions in supposed bribe money. 

Sounds promising, no? 

But you read deeper and you see the patterns that bother you. 

His outrage can look selective: loud in one corruption narrative, quiet in another where money is allegedly tied to an ally’s projects. 

Now there is news that he could face an ethics complaint himself over how he handled sensitive DPWH files tied to a former undersecretary who later died, with sources claiming those documents were taken forcibly. 

So on one side, may meow politics. On the other, curated outrage politics. 

Pareho silang bata. Pareho silang malakas ang dating online. Pareho ring questionable ang credibility at ethics. 🤷‍♂️

This is where the despair creeps in. 

 Rizal’s youth versus our influencers 

Rizal wrote “A la Juventud Filipina” to urge youth to use their talents in arts, science, and civic life to lift the country from oppression. 

The hope was not about age alone, it was about a certain quality of youth: educated, principled, willing to think of the country beyond themselves. 

The problem now is this: 

- The system rewards visibility more than virtue. Young politicians gain power by mastering social media, not by proving long-term service. 

- Parties and dynasties still decide who gets a shot. Being “pag-asa ng bayan” becomes more about surname, network, and PR than about track record and integrity. 

- Voters are tired and angry, so anything that looks like “galit sa corruption” feels enough, even if the details are messy, incomplete, or conveniently targeted. 

So we get youthful faces acting out very old patterns. New packaging, same traditional politics. 

And yet, Rizal’s line still echoes, because outside Congress, there are students, workers, campus journalists, and community organizers who take that quote seriously. 

Youth who study history, fight disinformation, join rallies, and insist that hindi kami lang ang future, kami na ang ngayon na bayan. 

We just do not always put them on TV. 

 What kind of youth we should actually be voting for 

If we expect more from the youth, then we have to be more specific about what “pag-asa” looks like in 2025 and beyond. 

For public life, youth worth betting on should look less like influencers and more like boring, steady workers with receipts. As in: 

- Consistent values, not just consistent engagement 
 
Someone who will call out corruption whether the suspect is enemy or ally, with the same intensity, and with documents, not just tears and mic drops. 

- Respect for institutions, even while criticizing them 
 
Yes, call for resignations if needed. But do not drag entire institutions into chaos for clout, or encourage the military to act in ways that blur democratic lines. 

- Transparent handling of power and information 
 
Sensitive files, leaks, and “whistleblowing” should follow ethical processes, not become props in a public performance that may put people or cases at risk. 

- Willingness to be boring 
 
Real reform work is committee hearings, long drafting sessions, consultations, coalition-building. The kind of work that never trends but changes lives. 

And honestly, youth leaders who know when to say “hindi ko alam, kailangan ko pang aralin yan” are far more hopeful than those who always have a viral line ready. 

 Our responsibility as the ones watching 

The country is not hopeless. It is just badly curated. 

Rizal did not say “ang kahit sinong batang may ring light ang pag-asa ng bayan.” The line has always assumed a filter: education, ethics, service, courage, and a willingness to put the country above ego. 

So hindi automatically “pag-asa ng bayan” ang kabataan. 

May qualifier si Rizal: the good and honest youth, the ones who actually give their time, talent, and future to the country, not to their brand. 

Kung may batang meow, batang anti-corruption kuno, batang dynasty baby, fine. They exist. 

Pero hindi sila template. 

Tayo pa rin ang magdedesisyon, every election, every share, every like, kung sino sa kabataan ang tatawagin nating pag-asa, at sino ang isa lang sa many, many warnings ng history para hindi tayo madaling mauto. 

Tonight, malinaw sa akin kung sino ang hindi.

I'm on a WestJet flight, en route to Los Angeles from Calgary, and I was just thinking about 2026 and how to move forward as MCT. 

Not in a grand way, but in a quiet way: sitting with my own doubts, wondering if this space is still useful, and asking what it should actually be doing in a country drowning in online noise. 

Because the more I look around, the clearer it feels that this can’t stay as “rant lang.” 

Not when lies are getting harder to spot and more people are openly worried about fake and misleading content online. 

If MCT continues in 2026, it can’t just be louder. It has to be clearer, calmer, and more intentional. 

 What this space has quietly been doing 

If you’ve been here the past year, you know this space hasn’t just been giving takes. It’s been quietly teaching. 

All this time, MCT has been showing how to ask better questions instead of swallowing the first version of the story. 

It has been nudging you to be picky with what you read and share. 

It has been using links, reports, and screenshots to help you connect dots, not just collect “tea.” 

It has been pushing you to keep asking deeper questions, even when the easy answer already feels satisfying. 

That is critical thinking, but in everyday form. 

It sits in how you scroll, what you click, what you ignore, and what you decide to pass on to others. 

It matters, because most people are meeting disinformation in the same place they get their news and relax: social media. 

So even a “small” blog like this becomes part of that same information world, whether it wants to or not. 

 What we’ve learned since 2016 

Since 2016, we have seen the same pattern again and again. 

Elections arrive. 

Lies and half-truths spread faster. 

Trolls, influencers, and “organic” fans share the same lines. 

People fight in the comments and in group chats. 

After that, everyone is tired and confused. 

We have also seen what does not work. 

Bardagulan made us feel brave for a while, but it mostly fed the algorithm and gave troll networks more content to use. 

The elitist tone, the “ang bobo niyo” mood, did not teach anyone; it just made the gap wider and confirmed the picture of “sila yung nagmamagaling.” 

Dismissive reactions, the “obvious namang fake ’yan” replies, were careless, because clearly, for many people, it was not obvious at all. 

Silence out of fear felt safe, but in a system built on lies, silence starts to look like agreement. 

During all this, the disinformation machine became more organized. 

Many campaigns are planned like ad campaigns, with teams, budgets, and timelines. 

They do not just show up during election month. They plan years ahead. 

So as we enter 2026, with 2028 already on the horizon, it is not honest to say “things will sort themselves out.” 

 The plan for 2026 

For 2026, MCT does not need to be bigger. It needs to be more clear about its work. 

The first part of the plan is to teach questions, not just opinions. Each post should leave you with simple, useful questions you can bring anywhere: how do I know this, who gains if I believe this, and what might be missing here. 

The second part is to make checking sources feel normal. When we talk about an issue, you will see links and context. Not to drown you in details, but to show that checking is something any person can do, not only experts. 

The third part is to connect patterns, not just react to events. Instead of chasing every viral scandal, this space will keep pointing out how the same tricks, stories, and networks keep coming back from 2016 to now. 

The fourth part is to drop the elitist energy for good. There is no place here for talking down to people or hinting that only a few are “smart enough.” The aim is to help more people understand, not to impress a small, already convinced group. 

The fifth part is to name what does not help. Bardagulan, mockery, and silence will not be treated as “strategy” anymore. They will be seen for what they are: ways we cope when we are angry or scared, but not ways that fix the problem. 

The sixth part is to prepare quietly for 2028. Every post in 2026 is part of a longer effort: making you harder to fool when the next wave of trolls, fake stories, and staged “movements” start warming up for the next national fight. 

 Accepting the limits of this space 

There is another honest part I cannot skip: MCT is a crowdfunded advocacy. 

It runs on time, energy, and work that real people choose to support. 

If one day the support slows down or stops, MCT may have to go quiet. 

If that happens, it will not be a scandal. 

It will simply mean this small corner has done what it could, for as long as it could. 

And that is okay. 

This page was never meant to carry the whole fight. 

Long before MCT existed, there were journalists, fact-checkers, teachers, organizers, researchers, and creators already pushing back against lies and helping people think. 

They will still be here even if this space has to fade. We are not the center of the advocacy. 

We are part of a much bigger line of people doing the same work in different ways. 

Knowing this makes the work in 2026 feel lighter and more focused. The goal is not to build something that must live forever. The goal is to use whatever time and support we still have to help you build habits and instincts that will stay with you, even if one day this page is gone. 

 What readers can expect from me 

From your side of the screen, this means a few things. 

You can expect posts that explain how to look at a story, not just what to feel about it. 

You can expect pieces that are slower and more reflective, not only reactions to whatever is trending in the last 24 hours. 

You can expect honest moments of “I don’t know yet” when the information is not complete. 

You can expect that when a take turns out to be wrong or unfair, it will be corrected, not quietly deleted and forgotten. 

The online world will keep changing. Lies will keep trying to look cleaner and more normal. 

But more people are also trying to double-check, to learn tools, to be more careful before they share. 

This blog wants to stand on that side: the side that is tired of being played and is ready to think a little deeper. 

 What I hope from you 

This cannot be a one-way thing. MCT cannot do this alone. 

In 2026, it would help a lot if readers send a message when something feels unclear, not to attack, but to ask for a source or an update. 

It helps when you share posts not only because they match your feelings, but because they also pass your own small checks.

It matters when you bring what you learn here into your own spaces, whether that is a family thread, an office chat, or a small group in your community. 

The other side has money, tools, and organized plans. What we have are habits, questions, and each other. 

So the plan for 2026 is simple. MCT will keep writing in a way that helps you think, not just react. 

It will keep connecting dots across years and elections. It will keep saying no to shortcuts that feel good for a moment but leave us just as lost. 

If there is one line to bring into the new year, maybe it is this: 

Don’t just consume information. Learn how to fight with it, think with it, and see through it.
